220	
221	\paragraph{(iii) The proof template for $W$-monoidality.} Mathlib's
222	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW} proves
223	\((J.W\,(A := A)).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) \emph{stalkwise} for presheaves valued in a
224	\emph{fixed} monoidal concrete category \(A\) (i.e.\ \(C^{op} \to A\)), via the
225	conservative-family characterisation \(\mathtt{hP.W\_iff}\),
226	\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.map\_tensor}\) and typeclass inference:
227	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W`
228	% and the `(J.W).IsMonoidal` instance, in
229	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean` (L48, L57)
230	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean).
231	% SOURCE QUOTE:
232	% "lemma ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W
233	%     [J.HasSheafCompose (forget A)] :
234	%     (J.W (A := A)).IsMonoidal :=
235	%   .mk' _ (fun f g hf hg ↦ by
236	%     simp only [hP.W_iff (A := A)] at hf hg ⊢
237	%     intro Φ
238	%     rw [Functor.Monoidal.map_tensor]
239	%     infer_instance)"
240	\textit{Source: [Mathlib],
241	\texttt{ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal\_W}, in
242	\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean}, L48--L57.}
243	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does
244	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a
245	\emph{varying} ring are not of the form \(C^{op} \to A\) for a fixed monoidal
246	\(A\).
247	
248	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
249	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
250	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
251	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
252	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
253	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
254	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
255	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
256	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
257	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
258	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
259	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
260	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
261	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
262	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
263	the \emph{full} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance or a coherent monoidal category.
264	The \emph{full monoidal-category} route~(e) is therefore off the critical path. The
265	associator does, however, consume a \emph{single} left-whisker existence lemma
266	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed by the d.2 stalk--tensor
267	commutation; so the stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) itself is on the critical path, even
268	though the full coherent monoidal category built from it is not.
269	
270	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
271	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
272	is already present in Mathlib: the file
273	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
274	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
275	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
276	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
277	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
278	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
279	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
280	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
281	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
282	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
283	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
284	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. The full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent monoidal category that route~(e) would
285	build is not pursued; but the (d.1)-linearity packaging and the (d.2) stalk--tensor
286	commutation are \emph{on} the critical path, since the associator's single open
287	left-whisker obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk} is closed
288	precisely by (d.2) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}). What is superseded is only the
289	\emph{pair}-of-whiskering-fields \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) packaging and the
290	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category, not the stalk infrastructure.
291	
292	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
293	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
294	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
295	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
296	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
297	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
298	independently of any upstream timing.
299	
300	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
301	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
302	
303	\begin{definition}
304	
305	
306	\leanok
307	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
308	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
310	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
311	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
312	  \[
313	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
314	  \]
315	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
316	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
317	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
318	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
319	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
320	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
321	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
322	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
323	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
324	  canonical compatibility
325	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
326	     \;\cong\;
327	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
328	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
329	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
330	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
331	
332	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
333	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
334	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
335	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
336	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
337	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
338	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
339	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
340	\end{definition}
341	
342	\begin{lemma}
343	
344	
345	\leanok
346	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
347	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
349	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
350	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
351	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
352	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
353	  \[
354	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
355	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
356	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
357	  \]
358	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
359	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
360	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
361	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
362	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
363	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
364	  \[
365	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
366	    \qquad
367	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
368	  \]
369	  the associator
370	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
371	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
372	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
373	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
374	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
375	\end{lemma}
376	
377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
379	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
380	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
381	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
382	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
383	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
384	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
385	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
386	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
387	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
388	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
389	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
390	\end{proof}
391	
392	\begin{remark}
393	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
394	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
395	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
396	        lem:whisker_of_W}
397	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
398	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
399	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
400	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
401	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
402	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
403	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
404	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
405	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
406	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
407	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
408	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
409	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
410	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
411	
412	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
413	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
414	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
415	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
416	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
417	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
418	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
419	  fixed-base presheaves in
420	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
421	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
422	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
423	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
424	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
425	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
426	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
427	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
428	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
429	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
430	
431	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
432	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
433	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
434	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
435	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
436	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
437	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
438	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
439	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
440	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
441	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
442	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
443	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
444	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
445	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
446	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
447	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
448	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
449	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
450	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
451	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
452	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
453	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
454	\end{remark}
455	
456	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
457	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
458	
459	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
460	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
461	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
462	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
463	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
464	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
465	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
466	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
467	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
468	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
469	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
470	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
471	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
472	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
473	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
474	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
475	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
476	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
477	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
478	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
479	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
480	to the off-path route~(e).
481	
482	\begin{lemma}
483	
484	\leanok
485	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
486	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
487	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
488	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
489	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
490	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
491	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
492	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
493	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
494	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
495	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
496	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
497	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
498	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
499	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
500	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
501	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
502	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
503	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
504	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
505	  \[
506	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
507	      \;\;:=\;\;
508	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
509	  \]
510	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
511	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
512	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
513	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
514	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
515	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
516	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
517	\end{lemma}
518	
519	\begin{proof}
520	
521	
522	\leanok
523	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
524	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
525	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
526	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
527	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
528	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
529	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
530	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
531	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
532	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
533	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
534	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
535	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
536	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
537	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
538	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
539	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
540	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
541	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
542	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
543	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
544	
545	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
546	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
547	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
548	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
549	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
550	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
551	\end{proof}
552	
553	\begin{lemma}
554	
555	
556	\leanok
557	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
558	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
559	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
560	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
561	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
562	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
563	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
564	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
565	  morphism
566	  \[
567	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
568	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
569	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
570	  \]
571	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
572	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
573	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
574	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
575	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
576	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
577	\end{lemma}
578	
579	\begin{proof}
580	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
581	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
582	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
583	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
584	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
585	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
586	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
587	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
588	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
589	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
590	  isomorphism}
591	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
592	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
593	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
594	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
595	  therefore extension of scalars along
596	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
597	
598	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
599	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
600	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
601	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
602	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
603	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
604	  the abstract pullback functor.
605	
606	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
607	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
608	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
609	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
610	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
611	   \;\cong\;
612	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
613	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
614	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
615	  comparison
616	  \[
617	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
618	      \;\longrightarrow\;
619	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
620	      \;\otimes\;
621	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
622	  \]
623	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
624	
625	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
626	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of
627	  % pullback/pushforward of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism of ringed
628	  % spaces is an adjoint pair)
629	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L252-273).
630	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
631	  % "\begin{lemma}
632	  % \label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}
633	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
634	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces.
635	  % \begin{enumerate}
636	  % \item The functor
637	  % $f_* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y)$
638	  % is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
639	  % \item The functor
640	  % $f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
641	  % is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
642	  %  [\dots]
643	  % \end{enumerate}
644	  % \end{lemma}
645	  %  [\dots]
646	  % Parts (1) and (2) hold because $(f^*, f_*)$ is an adjoint pair
647	  % of functors, see
648	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}"
649	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
650	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
651	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
652	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
653	
654	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
655	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
656	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
657	  adjoint of pushforward (the presheaf-of-modules avatar of the
658	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) adjunction of the cited Stacks lemma) and admits no
659	  sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned commit. The closure passes through
660	  two presheaf-level ingredients, of which exactly one now remains open.
661	
662	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
663	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
664	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
665	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
666	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
667	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
668	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
669	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
670	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
671	  \otimes_S N)\).
672	
673	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
674	  \[
675	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
676	  \]
677	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
678	  presheaf-level adjunction
679	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
680	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
681	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
682	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
683	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
684	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
685	  composing the resulting comparison with
686	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
687	
688	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
689	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
690	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
691	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
692	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
693	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
694	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
695	  presheaf level. This residual is being constructed \emph{directly}, mirroring the
696	  existing sheaf-level template app-for-app at the presheaf level (a Mathlib-gradient
697	  build, not a deferral to an upstream PR).
698	
699	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law for
700	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N\): the associator
701	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes the comparison
702	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\) on its sole consumer
703	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}, where the restriction is commuted
704	  past the \emph{sheafified} tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) \emph{before} any local
705	  trivialisation of \(M, N\) is introduced --- so the comparison is required for the
706	  global, untrivialised modules, and no free-cover specialisation can stand in for it.
707	  Consequently the H1 residual \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong
708	  \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is genuinely on the critical path, and closing it is
709	  the \emph{single open substrate obligation} of this chapter. The superseded
710	  route~(e) \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
711	  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) remains named only as a last-resort fallback should the
712	  presheaf-level adjunction itself bottom out; it is not the plan.
713	\end{proof}

715	\begin{lemma}
716	[Presheaf-level pushforward adjunction and strong-monoidal restriction (H1/H2 substrate)]
717	  \label{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}
718	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans,
719	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr,
720	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj,
721	        PresheafOfModules.isIso_of_isIso_app,
722	        PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}
723	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
724	  These five presheaf-level declarations are the two ingredients on which the
725	  linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} rests; both are absent from Mathlib at
726	  the pinned commit and are supplied project-side.
727	
728	  \emph{H1 (the pushforward adjunction).} For a morphism \(\beta\) of presheaves of
729	  rings, \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\) are the
730	  presheaf-level de-sheafifications of Mathlib's sheaf-level
731	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
732	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr}\)
733	  (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}),
734	  reproduced app-for-app one level down at the presheaf of modules. From them
735	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) assembles the presheaf-level adjunction
736	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), the
737	  presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
738	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\). Pairing this against the
739	  existing \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\) through
740	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) yields the canonical isomorphism
741	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) of two left
742	  adjoints of the same pushforward --- exactly the residual identified in Step~3 of
743	  the proof of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
744	
745	  \emph{H2 (strong-monoidal restriction).} \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_app}\) is the
746	  sectionwise isomorphism criterion: a morphism of presheaves of modules is an
747	  isomorphism once each of its section maps is. Built on it,
748	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}\) equips the presheaf-level
749	  restriction-of-scalars functor
750	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha\) with a \emph{strong}-monoidal
751	  structure whenever the base-change comparison \(\alpha\) is \emph{sectionwise
752	  bijective} --- the presheaf-level lift of the ModuleCat-level H2 core
753	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}, assembled section-by-section via
754	  \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\). It is to be distinguished from the
755	  module-level, ring-equivalence form
756	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\) already pinned in
757	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}: the present declaration is the
758	  \emph{presheaf}-level, \emph{bijective}-comparison form, the one the substrate lift
759	  actually consumes (where the comparison map
760	  \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)
761	  morphism but is not syntactically a \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\)).
762	
763	  Together H1 and H2 are the two Mathlib-absent ingredients the linchpin needed; they
764	  are self-contained presheaf-level results and are natural upstream-PR candidates.
765	\end{lemma}

767	\begin{lemma}
768	
769	\leanok
770	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
771	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
772	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
773	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
774	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
775	  product: the canonical map
776	  \[
777	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
778	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
779	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
780	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
781	  \]
782	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
783	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
784	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
785	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
786	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
787	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
788	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
789	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
790	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
791	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.
792	\end{lemma}
793	
794	\begin{lemma}
795	
796	[Strong-monoidal upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism (ModuleCat-level H2 core)]
797	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}
798	  \lean{restrictScalars_isIso_μ, restrictScalars_isIso_ε,
799	        restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv,
800	        restrictScalars_isIso_μ_of_bijective,
801	        restrictScalars_isIso_ε_of_bijective}
802	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
803	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings and let
804	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom} :
805	  \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,S \to \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R\) be the associated
806	  restriction-of-scalars functor, equipped with its canonical \emph{lax}-monoidal
807	  structure (the lax tensorator \(\mu\) and lax unit \(\varepsilon\) supplied by
808	  Mathlib). Then:
809	  \begin{enumerate}
810	    \item the lax tensorator
811	      \(\mu_{M, N} : \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e N
812	      \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M \otimes_S N)\) is an isomorphism
813	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu}\));
814	    \item the lax unit
815	      \(\varepsilon : \mathbf{1}_R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(\mathbf{1}_S)\)
816	      is an isomorphism (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon}\));
817	    \item consequently \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) is
818	      \emph{strong} monoidal: it carries the packaged structure
819	      \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
820	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\)).
821	  \end{enumerate}
822	  The same two invertibility statements hold, more flexibly, under the hypothesis that
823	  the underlying ring homomorphism is merely \emph{bijective} rather than literally of
824	  the form \(e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) for a packaged ring equivalence \(e\)
825	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu\_of\_bijective}\),
826	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon\_of\_bijective}\)); these bijective
827	  forms are the ones consumed by the presheaf-level lift, where the relevant
828	  comparison map \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective
829	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) morphism but is not syntactically a
830	  \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\).
831	\end{lemma}
832	
833	\begin{proof}
834	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
835	  Statements (1) and (2) are the categorical repackaging of
836	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}: when the ground-ring map is a ring
837	  \emph{isomorphism} the two ground rings are identified, so the lax structure maps
838	  \(\mu\) and \(\varepsilon\) of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) --- which are
839	  in general only one-directional --- become invertible. For (1) the inverse of
840	  \(\mu_{M, N}\) is the additive map \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\) of
841	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}, verified to be two-sided inverse to
842	  the canonical \(R\)-linear \(\mu_{M, N}\); for (2) the unit comparison
843	  \(\mathbf{1}_R = R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e S\) is the additive identification
844	  induced by \(e\). Strong monoidality (3) is then the Mathlib upgrade of a
845	  lax-monoidal functor all of whose structure maps are isomorphisms
846	  (\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\)), packaging \(\mu^{-1}, \varepsilon^{-1}\)
847	  into \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\).
848	  The bijective-hypothesis forms are proved identically, replacing ``\(e\) is a ring
849	  equivalence'' by ``the underlying ring homomorphism is bijective'' and reconstructing
850	  the inverse ground-ring map from bijectivity; this is what lets the presheaf-level
851	  lift feed in a section-wise-bijective comparison map directly.
852	
853	  These declarations constitute the \emph{ModuleCat-level H2}: strong
854	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism commutes with \(\otimes\). The
855	  one \emph{remaining} H2 step is the bounded, no-Mathlib-gap \emph{presheaf} lift:
856	  assembling \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
857	  section-by-section via \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\), using a
858	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-iso\,\(\Rightarrow\)\,bijective bridge to invoke the
859	  bijective forms above at each section, together with the
860	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) iso-iff-app-iso reflection to lift section-wise
861	  invertibility to the presheaf level; once that lift is in place,
862	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) is monoidal. This presheaf H2 lift is bounded and
863	  has no Mathlib-absent ingredient; the sole genuinely Mathlib-absent piece of
864	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is the presheaf-level pushforward adjunction
865	  (``H1'').
866	\end{proof}

868	\begin{lemma}
869	
870	
871	\leanok
872	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
873	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
874	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
875	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
876	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
877	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
878	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
879	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
880	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
881	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
882	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
883	\end{lemma}
884	
885	\begin{proof}
886	
887	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
888	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
889	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
890	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
891	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
892	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
893	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
894	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
895	\end{proof}

897	\emph{Closed standalone result --- off the critical path.} The following lemma
898	\emph{is} formalized and sorry-free; it is not, however, consumed by the relative
899	Picard group law. The associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is realized
900	\emph{unconditionally} through the varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation
901	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
902	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}), which closes the single left-whiskering
903	obligation for \emph{all} modules with no flatness and no local-triviality
904	hypothesis. The sectionwise-flatness whiskering statement below is therefore
905	neither the live whiskering-stability route nor on the critical path: it is a valid,
906	self-contained result about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms,
907	retained for potential reuse. (Unlike the genuinely abandoned
908	\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, it is a closed lemma, not a stub to be skipped.)
909	
910	\begin{lemma}
911	% Closed, sorry-free standalone result (flat whiskering). Off the critical path:
912	% the associator is realized via the d.2 stalk-tensor route
913	% (lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk), not via this flat lemma.
914	
915	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
916	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
917	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
918	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
919	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
920	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
921	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
922	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
923	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
924	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
925	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
926	  left-whiskered morphism
927	  \[
928	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
929	  \]
930	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
931	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
932	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
933	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
934	
935	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
936	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
937	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
938	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability obligation
939	  for the unconditional associator is closed for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering
940	  object \(F\) by the stalk--tensor commutation
941	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
942	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}), which never invokes the
943	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness
944	  condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the
945	  site, including non-affine opens --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility
946	  or local triviality, which are affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma
947	  is therefore retained as an independent, closed result, superseded on the critical
948	  path by the d.2 route of \cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}.
949	\end{lemma}
950	
951	\begin{proof}
952	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
953	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
954	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
955	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
956	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
957	
958	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
959	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
960	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
961	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
962	  \[
963	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
964	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
965	  \]
966	
967	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
968	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
969	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
970	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
971	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
972	
973	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
974	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
975	  linear maps (Mathlib
976	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
977	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
978	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
979	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
980	
981	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
982	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
983	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
984	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
985	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
986	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
987	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	\leanok
96	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
97	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
98	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
99	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
100	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
101	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
102	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
103	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
104	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
105	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
106	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
107	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
108	\end{proof}
109	
110	\begin{lemma}\leanok
111	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
112	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
114	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
115	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
116	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
117	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
118	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
119	  isomorphism.
120	\end{lemma}
121	
122	\begin{proof}
123	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
124	  \leanok
125	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
126	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
127	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
128	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
129	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
130	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
131	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
132	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
133	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
134	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
135	\end{proof}
136	
137	\begin{lemma}\leanok
138	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
139	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
140	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
141	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
142	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
143	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
144	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
145	\end{lemma}
146	
147	\begin{proof}
148	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
149	  \leanok
150	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
151	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
152	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
153	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
154	  affine opens.
155	\end{proof}
156	
157	\subsection{The affine computation}
158	
159	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
160	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
161	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
162	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
163	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
164	
165	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
166	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
167	their proof sketches.
168	
169	\begin{lemma}
170	
171	\leanok
172	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
173	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
175	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
176	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
177	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
178	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
179	  \[
180	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
181	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
182	  \]
183	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
184	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
185	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
186	\end{lemma}
187	
188	\begin{proof}
189	
190	\leanok
191	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
192	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
193	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
194	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
195	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
196	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
197	\end{proof}
198	
199	\begin{lemma}
200	
201	\leanok
202	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
203	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
205	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
206	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
207	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
208	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
209	  \[
210	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
211	    \;\cong\;
212	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
213	  \]
214	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
215	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
216	  of \(N\).
217	\end{lemma}
218	
219	\begin{proof}
220	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
221	  \leanok
222	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
223	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
224	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
225	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
226	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
227	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
228	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
229	  equation of
230	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
231	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
232	\end{proof}
233	
234	\begin{lemma}
235	
236	\leanok
237	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
238	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
240	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
241	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
242	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
243	  \[
244	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
245	    \;\cong\;
246	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
247	  \]
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
252	  \leanok
253	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
254	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
255	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
256	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
257	\end{proof}
258	
259	We record three further bespoke supports that drive the basic-open verification of
260	the affine-pushforward isomorphism below. They carry no external citation and stand
261	on their proof sketches.
262	
263	\begin{lemma}
264	
265	\leanok
266	[Restriction of scalars of a localized module]
267	  \label{lem:powers_restrictScalars}
268	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars}
269	  Let \(A\) be a commutative \(R\)-algebra, \(S \subseteq R\) a submonoid, and
270	  \(f : M \to N\) an \(A\)-linear map that exhibits \(N\) as the localization of
271	  \(M\) at the image submonoid
272	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S) \subseteq A\). Then the underlying \(R\)-linear map
273	  \(\operatorname{restr}_R f\) exhibits \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\)
274	  itself.
275	\end{lemma}
276	
277	\begin{proof}
278	
279	\leanok
280	  This is the exact converse of the standard fact that a localization of \(M\) at a
281	  submonoid \(S\) of \(R\) remains a localization after enlarging the base to an
282	  \(R\)-algebra \(A\) along the image submonoid \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\). One
283	  checks the three defining conditions of a localized module directly. Each
284	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(N\) because its image
285	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) does and the two actions coincide by the scalar
286	  tower \(R \to A \to N\). Surjectivity of \(f\) up to denominators and the
287	  equality-witness condition transport along the map
288	  \(s \mapsto \operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) from \(S\) onto
289	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\), again using
290	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\cdot{} = s\cdot{}\). In the application
291	  \(A = R'\), \(S = \mathrm{powers}(a)\), one has
292	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\), so
293	  this identifies the localization of \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at \(a\)
294	  with the localization of \(M\) at \(\varphi a\); it is the ring-change core of the
295	  basic-open computation below.
296	\end{proof}
297	
298	\begin{lemma}
299	
300	\leanok
301	[Section-level counit isomorphism from a localization hypothesis]
302	  \label{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.fromTildeΓ_app_isIso_of_isLocalizedModule}
304	  Let \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-module and \(a \in R\). Suppose the
305	  structure-sheaf restriction map \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\), read as
306	  an \(R\)-linear map of the global-section modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as
307	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then the
308	  tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit
309	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N : \widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)} \to N\) restricts to an
310	  isomorphism on the sections over the basic open \(D(a)\).
311	\end{lemma}
312	
313	\begin{proof}
314	
315	\leanok
316	  Over \(D(a)\) the section map \(L\) of the counit sits in the triangle identity
317	  \(L \circ j = \rho\), where
318	  \(j : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma\bigl(\widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)}, D(a)\bigr)\) is
319	  the tilde-localization map and \(\rho : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) is
320	  the structure-sheaf restriction. Both \(j\) and \(\rho\) exhibit their targets as
321	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N,\top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\) — \(j\) by the
322	  defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), and \(\rho\) by hypothesis. By the
323	  uniqueness of localized modules there is a unique \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\)
324	  with \(e \circ j = \rho\); the triangle identity then forces \(L = e\), so \(L\) is
325	  an isomorphism. (This is the per-basic-open input feeding the basis-local
326	  criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}.)
327	\end{proof}
328	
329	\begin{lemma}
330	
331	\leanok
332	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module, conditional form]
333	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional}
334	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule}
335	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
336	  lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
337	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and \(M\) an
338	  \(R'\)-module. Suppose that for every \(a \in R\) the structure-sheaf restriction
339	  of the pushforward \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) exhibits its
340	  sections over \(D(a)\) as the localization of its global sections at
341	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
342	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
343	  \[
344	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M \;\cong\;
345	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)} .
346	  \]
347	\end{lemma}
348	
349	\begin{proof}
350	  \leanok
351	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
352	  lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
353	  Write \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\). Under the hypothesis,
354	  Lemma~\ref{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized} applies for each \(a \in R\),
355	  so the counit \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) restricts to an isomorphism on the
356	  sections over every basic open \(D(a)\). Since the basic opens form a basis of
357	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), the basis-local criterion
358	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} upgrades this to: the counit
359	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) is an isomorphism, i.e.\ \(N\) lies in the
360	  essential image of \(\widetilde{(-)}\). Composing the inverse counit with the
361	  tilde of the global-sections comparison
362	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} yields the asserted object isomorphism.
363	\end{proof}
364	
365	\begin{lemma}
366	% NOTE: The three route-iii supports now have their own pinned blocks above —
367	% `lem:powers_restrictScalars` (ring-change core), `lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized`
368	% (section-level counit engine), and `lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional` (the assembly
369	% that takes the per-basic-open localization fact `hloc` as an explicit hypothesis). The
370	% unconditional decl named in the `\lean{}` pin below is the one remaining open obligation: it must
371	% supply `hloc` for `N := (Spec φ)_* M^~` and feed it to the conditional form. The sole difficulty
372	% in `hloc` is the structure-sheaf smul carrier wall over `D(a)`, whose element-free resolution is
373	% the `D(a)`-specialization of `lem:gammaPushforwardIso` spelled out in the proof below. Pin stays
374	% on the unconditional decl until the prover closes it.
375	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
376	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
377	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
378	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
379	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
380	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
381	  \[
382	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
383	    \;\cong\;
384	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
385	  \]
386	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
387	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
388	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
389	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
390	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
391	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
392	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
393	
394	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
395	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
396	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
397	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
398	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
399	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
400	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
401	\end{lemma}
402	
403	\begin{proof}
404	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
405	  lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars}
406	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
407	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
408	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
409	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
410	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
411	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
412	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
413	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
414	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
415	
416	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
417	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
418	  \[
419	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
420	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
421	  \]
422	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
423	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
424	
425	  \medskip
426	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
427	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
428	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
429	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
430	
431	  \medskip
432	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
433	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
434	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
435	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
436	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
437	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
438	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
439	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
440	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
441	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
442	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
443	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
444	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
445	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
446	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
447	  an isomorphism.
448	
449	  \medskip
450	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
451	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
452	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
453	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
454	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
455	  \(R[1/a]\); a naive section-level identity \(a \cdot x = \varphi(a) \cdot x\) does
456	  not present itself. The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
457	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
458	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
459	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
460	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
461	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). We construct \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the
462	  already-known \(R'\)-side localization, in element-free fashion, along a
463	  \(D(a)\)-level analogue of the \(\top\)-level identification
464	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}. This proceeds in three movements.
465	
466	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} In exact analogy to the
467	  \(\top\)-level Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, construct an \(R\)-linear
468	  isomorphism
469	  \[
470	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
471	      \;\cong\;
472	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) .
473	  \]
474	  Its construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim — two
475	  reconciliations of nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
476	  section maps, glued by a canonical isomorphism from the ring equation — except that the
477	  canonical isomorphism now rests on a \emph{\(D(a)\)-level} ring equation in place of
478	  the \(\top\)-level one of
479	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. The localization map
480	  \(R \to R[1/a]\) plays here the role that the global-section ring map plays at
481	  \(\top\).
482	
483	  \emph{(2) The \(D(a)\)-level ring equation.} The ring equation grounding this
484	  canonical isomorphism is the \(D(a)\)-shadow of
485	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}: writing
486	  \(g_R : R \to \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top)\) and
487	  \(g_{R'} : R' \to \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top)\) for the global-section
488	  ring maps and \(\mathrm{res}\) for the structure-sheaf restrictions,
489	  \[
490	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{D(a)}
491	      \circ \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} \circ g_R
492	    \;=\;
493	    \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} \circ g_{R'} \circ \varphi ,
494	  \]
495	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
496	  definitional. This is the sheaf-naturality of the structure-sheaf comparison map
497	  of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) evaluated at \(D(a)\); it states that the
498	  \(\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top)\)-action on the pushforward sections over
499	  \(D(a)\) factors, after restriction, through
500	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{D(a)}\).
501	
502	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target
503	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) = M[1/\varphi a]\) is canonically the
504	  \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\)
505	  over \(R'\). Apply the ring-change converse
506	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
507	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
508	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) to
509	  obtain the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\) of the same module,
510	  viewed by restriction of scalars. Transporting that localization property back
511	  across the linear equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement (1) — via the
512	  transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized modules — yields
513	  the localization property at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\) on
514	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). Feeding the family
515	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
516	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
517	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\). The element-free \(\top\)-level template is
518	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, and the \(D(a)\)-level construction above is
519	  its specialization with the localization map \(R \to R[1/a]\) in place of the
520	  global ring map.
521	
522	  \medskip
523	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
524	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
525	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
526	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
527	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
528	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
529	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
530	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
531	  needed for the affine lane.
532	\end{proof}
533	
534	\begin{remark}
535	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
536	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
537	  \begin{enumerate}
538	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
539	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
540	      \(R\)-module of sections of
541	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
542	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
543	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
544	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
545	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
546	      closed under isomorphism; hence
547	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
548	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
549	      needed for the affine lane.
550	  \end{enumerate}
551	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
552	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
553	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
554	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
555	\end{remark}

577	  Assume \(f : X \to S\) is an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a

579	  above the canonical map of
580	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
581	  \[

587	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module need not be of this form

589	  nor the tensor-associativity computation below is available, and the conclusion
