35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
70	
71	\begin{lemma}\leanok
72	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
73	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
74	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
75	  
76	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
77	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
78	  % divisors by an open subscheme of \(\IHilb_{X/S}\)); arXiv:math/0504020,
79	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
80	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
81	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
82	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
83	  %   Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat.  Then \(\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}\) is
84	  %  representable by an open subscheme \(\IDiv_{X/S}\) of the Hilbert scheme
85	  %  \(\IHilb_{X/S}\).
86	  %   \end{thm}"
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
88	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
89	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
90	  %      \(\)A_{X/S}(T)\:\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)\(\)
91	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor \(D\) on \(X_T/T\) to the
92	  %  sheaf \(\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\).  The target \(\Pic_{X/S}\) may be replaced by any
93	  %  of its associated sheaves."
94	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
95	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
96	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
98	  \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\)
99	  \[
100	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et},
101	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
102	  \]
103	  where \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
104	  on \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), and the target is the
105	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of
106	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The functor \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) is representable
107	  by an open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}\) of the Hilbert scheme
108	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) the special case
109	  \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
110	  representable by a morphism of \(k\)-schemes $A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to
111	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
115	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
116	% "Set \(H:=\IHilb_{X/S}\), and let \(W\subset X\x H\) be the universal
117	% (closed) subscheme, and \(q\:W\to H\) the projection.  Let \(V\) denote the
118	% set of points \(w\in W\) at which \(W\) is an effective divisor. Plainly \(V\)
119	% is open in \(W\).  Set \(Z:=q(W-V)\).  Then \(Z\) is closed because \(q\) is
120	% proper.  Set \(U:=H-Z\).  Then \(U\) is open, and \(q^{-1}U\) is an effective
121	% divisor in \(X\x U\).  In fact, since \(q\) is flat, \(q^{-1}U\) is a relative
122	% effective divisor in \(X\x U/U\).
123	% [\dots]"
124	\begin{proof}
125	  
126	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
127	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\( is representable by a \)k$-scheme; this uses
128	  \(C/k\) projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
129	  stronger). Let \(W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}\) be the universal
130	  closed subscheme and \(q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}\) the projection. The locus
131	  \(V \subseteq W\) where \(W\) is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
132	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement \(W \setminus V\)
133	  is closed in \(W\), and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
134	  \Hilb_{C/k}\( is closed because \)q\( is proper. Hence \)\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} :=
135	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z\( is open in \)\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
136	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) the
137	  restriction of \(W\) is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
138	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
139	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
140	  \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} / \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}$.
141	
142	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor \(D \subseteq C \times_k T\)
143	  to the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}\)
144	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
145	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in \(T\): pullback of an ideal
146	  sheaf along \(\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\)
147	  takes the ideal of \(D\) to the ideal of \(D_{T'}\) (since \(D\) is flat over
148	  \(T\), the schematic inverse image \(D_{T'}\) is a relative effective
149	  divisor on \(C \times_k T'/T'\), again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
150	  takes \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})\).
151	  Composing with the canonical projection
152	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of
153	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
154	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
155	  natural map \(A_{C/k}(T) : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
156	\end{proof}
157	
158	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
159	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
160	
161	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
162	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
163	of Route~A: \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral
164	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
165	\(X/S\) projective Zariski-locally over \(S\), flat, with integral geometric
166	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
167	
168	\begin{theorem}\leanok
169	[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
170	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
172	  
173	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main,
174	  % and Cor.~cor:algsch (specialisation to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X\) complete);
175	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~26, 35 (read from
176	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).
177	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
178	  % "\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
179	  %   Assume \(f\:X\to S\) is projective Zariski locally over \(S\), and is flat
180	  %  with integral geometric fibers.
181	  %
182	  %  \tu{(1)} Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists, is separated and locally of finite
183	  %  type over \(S\), and represents \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).
184	  %
185	  %  \tu{(2)} If also \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective, then
186	  %  \(\IPic_{X/S}\) is a disjoint union of open
187	  %   subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
188	  %  \(S\)-schemes.
189	  %   \end{thm}"
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
191	  % "\begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
192	  %    Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\), and \(X\) is complete.  Then
193	  %  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists, represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\), and is a disjoint
194	  %  union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
195	  %   \end{sbscor}"
196	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main
197	  + Cor.~cor:algsch.}
198	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
199	  over \(k\). Then the \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor
200	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is
201	  representable by a \(k\)-scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), separated and locally of
202	  finite type over \(k\), which is a disjoint union of open
203	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes, each an increasing union of open
204	  quasi-projective subschemes. The scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is called the
205	  \emph{Picard scheme} of \(C/k\).
206	\end{theorem}
207	
208	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
209	% L2168-L2366, proof of th:main; long and is broken into the four-step
210	% sketch below per the project's structural restatement convention. The
211	% verbatim quote of the opening reduction step is:)
212	% "By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on \(S\) to
213	% represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of \(S\)-schemes.  Moreover, it
214	% is also a local matter on \(S\) to prove that an \(S\)-scheme is separated
215	% and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
216	% \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective.
217	% [\dots]
218	% Plainly an \(S\)-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
219	% separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
220	% separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2)."
221	% (subsequent steps quoted inline in the project-notation proof below).
222	\begin{proof}
223	  
224	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:main, specialised to \(S = \Spec k\) and
225	  \(X = C\). The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps; the underlying
226	  algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
227	  and the \'etale-sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
228	  together with the line-bundle / Quot correspondence of
229	  \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
230	
231	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
232	  For each polynomial \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\), let $P^\phi \subseteq
233	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\( be the \'etale subsheaf whose \)T$-points are
234	  represented by invertible sheaves \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) with
235	  \(\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)\) for all \(t \in T\) (the
236	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
237	  \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on the projective curve \(C\)). The \(P^\phi\) form a
238	  disjoint open cover of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (openness via Kleiman's
239	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
240	  represent each \(P^\phi\) by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
241	  \(k\)-schemes.
242	
243	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by \(m\)-regularity.}
244	  Given \(\phi\), fix \(m\) large enough that the \(T\)-points of \(P^\phi\) are
245	  representable by \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) satisfying
246	  \(R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\), \(n \ge m\). Twisting
247	  by \(\mathcal{O}_C(m)\) defines an automorphism of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
248	  that carries \(P^\phi\) to \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) for \(\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)\).
249	  Hence it suffices to represent each \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) by a quasi-projective
250	  \(k\)-scheme.
251	
252	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
253	  Consider the Abel map \(A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
254	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
255	  is representable by an open subscheme of \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) (and hence by an
256	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
257	  is quasi-projective over \(k\). Pulling back along
258	  \(P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) yields an open
259	  subscheme \(Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
260	  of \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\), which is again quasi-projective. The projection
261	  \(\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
262	  given a \(T\)-point \(\lambda\) of \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) represented by an \'etale
263	  cover \(T' \to T\) and an invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}'\) on $C \times_k
264	  T'\( with \)H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
265	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'\) associated to
266	  \(\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'\) is smooth over \(T'\); an \'etale
267	  cover \(T_1 \to T'\) admits a \(T'\)-map \(T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})\)
268	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
269	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z\) produces the required \(T_1\)-point of \(Z\).
270	  Moreover, the first projection \(Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z\) is
271	  smooth and proper.
272	
273	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
274	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
275	  of \'etale sheaves with \(Z\) quasi-projective, \(R := Z \times_P Z\)
276	  representable, and the first projection \(R \to Z\) smooth and proper,
277	  \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
278	  \(P = P^{\phi_0}_0\), \(Z\) and \(R\) as above, so \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) is
279	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\) and
280	  over \(m\), the disjoint union of representable \(P^\phi\) pieces represents
281	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\). The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
282	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
283	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the \(S = \Spec k\),
284	  \(X = C\) complete case.
285	\end{proof}
286	
287	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
288	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
289	
290	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
291	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
292	abstract content allowing the descent from \(Z = \mathrm{Div}^{\phi_0}_0\) to the
293	representing Picard piece.
294	
295	\begin{lemma}\leanok
296	[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
297	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
299	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
300	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
301	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
302	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
303	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
304	  %   Let \(\alpha\:Z\to P\) be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set \(R:=Z\x_PZ\).
305	  %  Assume \(\alpha\) is a surjection, \(Z\) is representable by a
306	  %  quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, \(R\) is representable by an \(S\)-scheme, and
307	  %  the first projection \(R\to Z\) is representable by a smooth and proper
308	  %  map.  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, and
309	  %  \(\alpha\) is representable by a smooth map."
310	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt.}
311	  Let \(\alpha : Z \to P\) be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on \((\Sch/k)\).
312	  Set \(R := Z \times_P Z\). Suppose:
313	  \begin{enumerate}
314	    \item \(\alpha\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
315	    \item \(Z\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme;
316	    \item \(R\) is representable by a \(k\)-scheme;
317	    \item the first projection \(R \to Z\) is representable by a smooth
318	      and proper map.
319	  \end{enumerate}
320	  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, and \(\alpha\)
321	  is representable by a smooth map.
322	\end{lemma}
323	
324	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
325	% L2377-L2417, proof of lm:qt; reproduced verbatim because it is the
326	% structural heart of step 4.)
327	% "To lighten the notation, let \(Z\) and \(R\) denote the
328	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map \(Z\to S\) is
329	% quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
330	% \(Z\x_SZ\to Z\) is too.  But the first projection \(R\to Z\) is proper, and
331	% factors naturally through a map \(h\:R\to Z\x_SZ\).  Hence \(h\) is proper.
332	% But \(h\) is a monomorphism; that is, \(h\) is injective on \(T\)-points.
333	% Therefore, \(h\) is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
334	%
335	% Plainly, for each \(S\)-scheme \(T\), the subset $R(T)\subset
336	% Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
337	% map of schemes \(R\to Z\) is flat and proper, and \(Z\) is a
338	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
339	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat and projective map
340	% \(Z\to Q\) such that \(R=Z\x_QZ\).  (In other words, a flat and proper
341	% equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)
342	% [\dots]
343	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
344	% smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
345	% same as those of \(R\to Z\).  And \(R\to Z\) is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
346	% \(Z\to Q\) is smooth.
347	%
348	% It remains to see that \(Q\) represents \(P\).  First, observe that \(Z\to Q\)
349	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
350	% [\dots]
351	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, \(Q\) is, in the
352	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
353	% \(R\rightrightarrows Z\) by Exercise~REF below, which is an
354	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
355	% exercise, \(P\) too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
356	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus \(Q\)
357	% represents \(P\)."
358	\begin{proof}
359	
360	\leanok
361	  By (ii) the structure map \(Z \to \Spec k\) is quasi-projective, hence
362	  separated, so the first projection \(Z \times_k Z \to Z\) is separated.
363	  By (iv) the first projection \(R \to Z\) is proper; it factors through
364	  \(h : R \to Z \times_k Z\) over the diagonal embedding (the second
365	  component is the second projection of \(R \to Z \times_P Z\)). The map
366	  \(h\) is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because \(R\) is the graph
367	  of an equivalence relation on \(Z\), hence injective on \(T\)-points).
368	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: \(h\) is a
369	  closed embedding.
370	
371	  Hence \(R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z\) is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
372	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Z\). By the
373	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
374	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
375	  the graph \(R \subseteq Z \times_k Z\) inside the universal subscheme of
376	  the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{Z/k}\) and descends to a closed subscheme
377	  \(Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}\) representing the quotient), there exist a
378	  quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat, projective map
379	  \(Z \to Q\) with \(R = Z \times_Q Z\).
380	
381	  The map \(Z \to Q\) is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
382	  extension, coincide with those of \(R \to Z\), which are smooth by
383	  hypothesis. Hence \(Z \to Q\) is smooth.
384	
385	  Finally, \(Z \to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
386	  cover of any \(T\)-point of \(Q\) pulls back to a \(T'\)-point of \(Z\), by
387	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to \(Z \times_Q T \to T\)). In the category
388	  of \'etale sheaves, both \(Q\) and \(P\) are coequalisers of the pair
389	  \(R \rightrightarrows Z\); the coequaliser is unique up to unique
390	  isomorphism, so \(Q\) represents \(P\). Smoothness of \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
391	  is the smoothness of \(Z \to Q\) just shown.
392	\end{proof}
393	
394	\section{Group-scheme structure}
395	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
396	
397	The Picard scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) inherits an abelian group structure from
398	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The relative
399	Picard presheaf \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\) is already an abelian
400	group at every \(T\) (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
401	functorially in \(T\); this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
402	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.
403	
404	\begin{theorem}\leanok
405	[\(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group scheme]
406	  \label{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
408	  
409	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs (the relative
410	  % Picard functor takes values in abelian groups) and \S 4 surrounding
411	  % text (group-scheme structure on \(\IPic_{X/S}\) via tensor product);
412	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~17, 26 (read from
413	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318 + L2057-L2069).
414	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
415	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
416	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
417	  %      \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
418	  %   Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
419	  %  topologies  by
420	  %      \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
421	  %      \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
422	  %   \end{dfn}"
423	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
424	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
425	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
426	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
427	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
428	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
429	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs +
430	  \S 4, Def.~df:Psch (the Picard scheme).}
431	  Let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
432	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is canonically an
433	  abelian group scheme over \(k\): the tensor product
434	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]\)
435	  and inverse \(-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]\) on
436	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) (well defined because both operations
437	  preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup
438	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined})
439	  are natural in \(T\), hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms
440	  \(m : \Pic_{C/k} \times_k \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\),
441	  \(i : \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\), and
442	  \(e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}\) (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying
443	  the abelian-group axioms. In particular, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group
444	  scheme locally of finite type.
445	\end{theorem}
446	
447	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- Kleiman \S 2--4 do not isolate a
448	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in
449	% the fact that \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) takes values in abelian groups (df:Pfs)
450	% and that representability transfers algebraic operations from set-valued
451	% functors to scheme morphisms by Yoneda. The structural restatement below
452	% records the standard transfer.
453	\begin{proof}
454	  
455	  The relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
456	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is already abelian-group
457	  valued, with sum $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] = [\mathcal{L} \otimes
458	  \mathcal{M}]\(, inverse \)-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
459	  \(0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\); these operations descend through
460	  the quotient by \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) because \(\pi_T^*\) is itself a
461	  group homomorphism. The \'etale sheafification step of
462	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} preserves the abelian-group structure
463	  on values, because sheafification of an abelian-presheaf is again an
464	  abelian-sheaf (the sheafification functor commutes with finite products
465	  in the value category).
466	
467	  By \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is
468	  represented by the scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The Yoneda embedding is fully
469	  faithful and preserves limits, so the natural transformations
470	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \times \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to
471	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (multiplication),
472	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (inverse), and the
473	  unit map \(h_{\Spec k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) pick out unique
474	  morphisms of representing schemes \(m\), \(i\), \(e\) as displayed. The
475	  abelian-group axioms (associativity, commutativity, identity,
476	  inverse) hold at the level of \(T\)-points functorially in \(T\), hence
477	  hold on the representing scheme by Yoneda. The local-finite-type
478	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
479	\end{proof}
480	
481	\section{The Picard scheme: the named declaration}
482	\label{sec:fga_pic_named_definition}
483	
484	The two preceding theorems are existence statements. We record the
485	named declaration corresponding to the Lean target.
486	
487	\begin{definition}\leanok
488	[The Picard scheme]
489	  \label{def:pic_scheme}
490	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
491	  
492	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch;
493	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26 (read from
494	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2057-L2062).
495	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
496	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
497	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
498	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
499	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
500	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
501	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch.}
502	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
503	  \(k\). The \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is the \(k\)-scheme representing
504	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\), supplied by
505	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, equipped with the abelian-group-scheme
506	  structure of \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}. The scheme is separated,
507	  locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of open
508	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes.
509	\end{definition}
510	
511	\section{Lean encoding}
512	\label{sec:fga_pic_lean_encoding}
513	
514	The Lean target file is the new
515	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}, sibling to
516	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
517	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) and
518	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}
519	(\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}). The blueprint declarations of
520	this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
521	
522	\begin{itemize}
523	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
524	    (\cref{def:pic_scheme}) is a structural constant naming the scheme
525	    representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\); it is the witness extracted
526	    from the \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy} produced by
527	    \texttt{representable} below.
528	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
529	    (\cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}) is the morphism of
530	    \(k\)-schemes \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) sending a
531	    relative effective divisor to its associated invertible sheaf.
532	    The construction uses the Hilbert / Quot scheme produced by
533	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (named
534	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme} per the directive's
535	    sibling-chapter naming), restricts to the open subscheme of relative
536	    effective divisors via the Kleiman~\S 3 effective-divisor open
537	    criterion, and composes with the quotient map to
538	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\).
539	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
540	    (\cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}) is the Altman--Kleiman
541	    descent of a flat-and-proper equivalence relation $R \subseteq Z
542	    \times_k Z\( on a quasi-projective \)k\(-scheme \)Z$ to a
543	    quasi-projective quotient \(Q\), packaged in the form that consumes
544	    a \(\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\)-witness for \(Z\) and produces
545	    one for the coequalising sheaf.
546	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
547	    (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}) is the assembly: from the
548	    \texttt{abelMap}, the \texttt{QuotScheme} representability witness,
549	    and the \texttt{rel\_pic\_etale\_sheafification} of
550	    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, build the
551	    \(\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\)-witness for
552	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) via the four steps of the proof above
553	    (Hilbert-polynomial stratification, \(m\)-regularity bound, Abel-map
554	    factorisation, smooth-proper quotient).
555	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
556	    (\cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}) is the
557	    \texttt{CommGroupObject} (or
558	    \texttt{AddCommGrp\_Object}) instance on \texttt{PicScheme} obtained
559	    by Yoneda transport of the abelian-group structure on
560	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\).
561	\end{itemize}

3638	  the \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear morphisms \(M|_U \to N|_U\) of presheaves of
3639	  modules on \(U\), formed via the open-immersion restriction \(U \hookrightarrow X\).
3640	  This value module is the slice specialisation
3641	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value} of the morphism module
3642	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}.
3643	
3644	  Explicitly, \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules assembled from
3645	  three pieces. \emph{(a) Value modules.} On each open \(U\) the value is the
3646	  \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} /
3647	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}. \emph{(b) Restriction maps.} For an
3648	  inclusion \(V \subseteq U\) the restriction map
3649	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \to \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V)\) is the further-restriction map
3650	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}, \(\varphi \mapsto \varphi|_V\), which
3651	  is additive and semilinear over the ring restriction \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\)
3652	  induced by \(g : V \to U\) --- the compatibility datum that the presheaf-of-modules
3653	  assembly (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.mk}\), via \(\mathtt{ofPresheaf}\)) consumes.
3654	  \emph{(c) Functoriality.} Restriction along the identity \(U \to U\) is the identity
3655	  map, and restriction along a composite \(V \xrightarrow{g} U \xrightarrow{h} W\) of
3656	  inclusions \(V \subseteq U \subseteq W\) (the morphism \(g : V \to U\) followed by
3657	  \(h : U \to W\) in \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\)) is the composite of the two
3658	  restriction maps \(\mathcal{H}om(M,N)(W) \to \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(U) \to
3659	  \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(V)\); both identity and composition are inherited from the
3660	  functoriality of further restriction, since \(\mathtt{Over.map}\) is a functor. These three pieces
3661	  make \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) a genuine, covariant presheaf of \(R\)-modules; this
3662	  assembled presheaf is the construction target \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.internalHom}\).
3663	
3664	  The slice formula is forced by contravariance: the pointwise rule
3665	  \(U \mapsto \Hom_{R(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) varies \emph{against} the restriction maps of
3666	  \(M\) and so is not a covariant presheaf, whereas the module of morphisms of
3667	  \emph{restricted} objects \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricts covariantly. Thus, unlike the
3668	  tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} --- which is covariant in the
3669	  restriction maps and sheafifies directly --- the internal hom must be built through
3670	  the slice presentation, and there is no presheaf-level dual to sheafify naively.
3671	\end{definition}
3672	
3673	\begin{lemma}
3674	
3675	\leanok
3676	[Restriction map of the presheaf internal hom]
3677	  \label{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
3678	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.restrictionMap}
3679	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value, def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
3680	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
3681	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
3682	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3508).
3683	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3684	  % "Consider the rule
3685	  % $$
3686	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
3687	  % $$
3688	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
3689	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
3690	  % abelian groups."
3691	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
3692	  (tag area 01CM): the rule \(U \mapsto \Hom(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)\), with its
3693	  further-restriction maps, is a (pre)sheaf of abelian groups.}
3694	  Let \(g : V \to U\) be a morphism of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\) (an inclusion
3695	  \(V \subseteq U\) of opens), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). There
3696	  is a restriction map
3697	  \[
3698	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \longrightarrow \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V),
3699	    \qquad \varphi \longmapsto \varphi|_V,
3700	  \]
3701	  the \(\mathtt{restrictionMap}\), sending a morphism of restricted modules
3702	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to its further restriction \(M|_V \to N|_V\). Concretely, the
3703	  functor \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g : \mathcal{C}/V \to \mathcal{C}/U\) realises
3704	  ``restrict from \(U\) to \(V\)'', and \(\varphi|_V\) is \(\varphi\) pulled back along
3705	  it --- the further restriction of the same morphism of presheaves of modules. This
3706	  map is additive, and it is \emph{semilinear} over the ring restriction
3707	  \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\): for \(f \in R(U)\),
3708	  \[
3709	    (f \cdot \varphi)\big|_V \;=\; R(g)(f) \cdot \bigl(\varphi|_V\bigr).
3710	  \]
3711	  This semilinearity is the compatibility datum that the presheaf assembly of
3712	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} (through \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf}\) /
3713	  \(\mathtt{mk}\)) requires.
3714	\end{lemma}
3715	
3716	\begin{proof}
3717	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
3718	  \leanok
3719	  Further restriction along \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g\) is functorial, so it carries a
3720	  morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to a morphism \(M|_V \to N|_V\) and respects identities and
3721	  composites; in particular the induced map on morphism groups is additive. For the
3722	  semilinearity, recall that the global-scalar action of
3723	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} acts on \(\varphi\) by post-composition with
3724	  the multiplication-by-\(f\) endomorphism \(m_f^{N}\); restricting that endomorphism
3725	  from \(U\) to \(V\) is multiplication by \(f|_V = R(g)(f)\) on \(N|_V\). Since further
3726	  restriction commutes with composition of morphisms, restricting \(f \cdot \varphi =
3727	  m_f^{N} \circ \varphi\) yields \(m_{R(g)(f)}^{N|_V} \circ (\varphi|_V) = R(g)(f) \cdot
3728	  (\varphi|_V)\), which is the asserted compatibility.
3729	\end{proof}

3731	\begin{definition}
3732	
3733	\leanok
3734	[Presheaf dual against the structure presheaf]
3735	  \label{def:presheaf_dual}
3736	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dual}
3737	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
3738	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as in \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}, the
3739	  \emph{presheaf dual} of \(M\) is the internal hom into the unit (structure)
3740	  presheaf,
3741	  \[
3742	    M^\vee \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om(M, R),
3743	  \]
3744	  i.e.\ \(M^\vee(U) = \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\,(R(U))\,(M|_U \to R|_U)\), the
3745	  \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\). This is the
3746	  presheaf-of-modules analogue of the linear dual
3747	  \(\mathtt{Module.Dual}\,R\,M = (M \to R)\) that serves as the \(\otimes\)-inverse of
3748	  an invertible module over a fixed ring; here the fixed-ring dual is taken
3749	  open-by-open and assembled by the slice presentation of
3750	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}.
3751	\end{definition}

3753	\begin{lemma}
3754	
3755	\leanok
3756	[Evaluation/contraction of the internal hom]
3757	  \label{lem:internal_hom_eval}
3758	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.internalHomEval}
3759	  % NOTE (iter-224): \texttt{PresheafOfModules.internalHomEval} is CLOSED axiom-clean
3760	  % (\(\{\texttt{propext}, \texttt{Classical.choice}, \texttt{Quot.sound}\}\)). The full
3761	  % morphism assembles the per-open contraction \texttt{internalHomEvalApp}
3762	  % (the \(R(U)\)-bilinear \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\),
3763	  % \(s \otimes \varphi \mapsto \varphi(s)\)) sectionwise; naturality follows from the
3764	  % evaluate/restrict-commutation argument in the proof body. The iter-222/223 ``whnf
3765	  % heartbeat-bomb'' obstacle was a stale Mathlib-version artifact and is gone.
3766	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
3767	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
3768	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
3769	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3517--L3524).
3770	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3771	  % "There is a canonical ``evaluation'' morphism
3772	  % $$
3773	  % \mathcal{F}
3774	  % \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
3775	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
3776	  % \longrightarrow
3777	  % \mathcal{G}.
3778	  % $$"
3779	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
3780	  (tag area 01CM).}
3781	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as above, there is a canonical \emph{evaluation} (contraction)
3782	  morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
3783	  \[
3784	    \mathrm{ev}_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_R M^\vee \;\longrightarrow\; R,
3785	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
3786	  \]
3787	  the counit of the tensor--hom adjunction
3788	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M \otimes_R -, R) \cong \mathcal{H}om(-, M^\vee)\) specialised at
3789	  \(R\). On each open \(U\) it is the open-by-open contraction
3790	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R|_U} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R|_U\), \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto
3791	  \phi(s)\), and these are compatible with restriction, so they assemble to a morphism
3792	  of presheaves of modules. The tensor product is the presheaf tensor underlying
3793	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
3794	\end{lemma}
3795	
3796	\begin{proof}
3797	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
3798	  \leanok
3799	  On a fixed open \(U\), the value \(M^\vee(U) = (M|_U \to R|_U)\) is the module of
3800	  \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\), and the evaluation
3801	  \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto \phi(s)\) is the standard contraction
3802	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\) bilinear over \(R(U)\), hence a
3803	  well-defined \(R(U)\)-linear map out of the tensor product. For \(V \subseteq U\)
3804	  the square relating the \(U\)- and \(V\)-contractions commutes because evaluating
3805	  then restricting equals restricting both arguments then evaluating
3806	  (\(\phi(s)|_V = (\phi|_V)(s|_V)\)); hence the open-by-open contractions are natural
3807	  in the restriction maps and define a morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules.
3808	  Concretely, the morphism is defined by specifying, on each open \(U\), the per-open
3809	  contraction \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\), \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto \phi(s)\).
3810	  The single remaining datum is the naturality square, which reduces to the
3811	  evaluate/restrict-commutation identity \(\phi(s)|_V = (\phi|_V)(s|_V)\) above: the
3812	  restriction \(\phi|_V\) of a functional inside the dual \(M^\vee\) is its restriction
3813	  to the open \(V\), and restriction is functorial, so evaluating the restricted
3814	  functional on the restricted section agrees with restricting the value of the
3815	  evaluation. This is the same restriction-functoriality argument already used to make
3816	  the internal-hom restriction maps functorial in
3817	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}. This is the counit of the tensor--hom
3818	  adjunction of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} specialised to the unit presheaf.
3819	\end{proof}

3821	\begin{lemma}
3822	
3823	
3824	\leanok
3825	[The internal hom is a sheaf; the sheaf-level dual]
3826	  \label{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}
3827	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual}
3828	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
3829	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
3830	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
3831	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3514).
3832	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3833	  % "Consider the rule
3834	  % $$
3835	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
3836	  % $$
3837	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
3838	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
3839	  % abelian groups. ... Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
3840	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
3841	  (tag area 01CM).}
3842	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), and let
3843	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with \(N\) a sheaf of modules. Then the
3844	  presheaf internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
3845	  satisfies the sheaf condition: for an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\), a family of
3846	  morphisms \(M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) agreeing on the overlaps
3847	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) glues to a unique morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\), because \(N\) is a
3848	  sheaf and morphisms of sheaves of modules are determined and gluable section-wise.
3849	  Hence \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) descends to an object of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
3850	  Specialising to \(N = \mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,R\) gives the
3851	  \emph{sheaf-level dual}
3852	  \[
3853	    \mathtt{dual}\,M \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X)
3854	    \;\in\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X,
3855	  \]
3856	  and the evaluation \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval} descends to a morphism of sheaves of
3857	  modules \(M \otimes_X \mathtt{dual}\,M \to \mathcal{O}_X\).
3858	\end{lemma}
3859	
3860	\begin{proof}
3861	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
3862	  Fix an open \(U\) and an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\). A morphism
3863	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) is a section of \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) over \(U\); restricting it
3864	  to the \(U_i\) gives a compatible family. Conversely, given morphisms
3865	  \(\varphi_i : M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) with \(\varphi_i|_{U_i \cap U_j} =
3866	  \varphi_j|_{U_i \cap U_j}\), section-wise gluing in the sheaf \(N\) produces, for
3867	  each open \(W \subseteq U\) and section \(s \in M(W)\), a well-defined section of
3868	  \(N(W)\) by gluing the \(\varphi_i(s|_{W \cap U_i})\) (these agree on overlaps by
3869	  hypothesis, and \(N\) is a sheaf so the glued section is unique). The resulting
3870	  assignment is \(R(W)\)-linear and natural in \(W\), hence a morphism
3871	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricting to the \(\varphi_i\); uniqueness is the separatedness
3872	  of \(N\). This is exactly the sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\), so it is a
3873	  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Taking \(N = \mathcal{O}_X\) yields
3874	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,M\), and the evaluation morphism of \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval},
3875	  being a morphism of presheaves into the sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\), is a morphism of
3876	  the corresponding sheaves of modules.
3877	\end{proof}

3879	\begin{lemma}
3880	
3881	
3882	\leanok
3883	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with the linear dual (H2$'$)]
3884	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
3885	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoDualEquiv}
3886	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
3887	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M\) be an
3888	  \(S\)-module. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the formation of the
3889	  linear dual: the canonical map
3890	  \[
3891	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \to_S S\bigr)
3892	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
3893	    \bigl(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M \to_R R\bigr),
3894	    \qquad \varphi \;\longmapsto\; e^{-1} \circ \varphi,
3895	  \]
3896	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence, with inverse \(\psi \mapsto e \circ \psi\). On both
3897	  sides the \(R\)-module structure is the \(S\)-module structure of \(M\) pulled back
3898	  along \(e\), so that \(r \cdot m = e(r) \cdot m\): the left-hand side is the
3899	  \(S\)-linear dual \((M \to_S S)\) regarded as an \(R\)-module by restriction, and the
3900	  right-hand side is the \(R\)-linear dual of the restricted module
3901	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M\) into the restricted ground ring. This is the dual
3902	  analogue of \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}: it is to the internal hom
3903	  / dual exactly what that lemma is to the tensor product. It is the \emph{H2$'$
3904	  ingredient} of the restriction-compatibility isomorphism of the C-bridge
3905	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} --- the ring-iso/dual commutation that, composed with
3906	  the open-immersion pushforward comparison H1, identifies the dual of a restriction
3907	  with the restriction of the dual.
3908	\end{lemma}
3909	
3910	\begin{proof}
3911	
3912	\leanok
3913	  The two assignments \(\varphi \mapsto e^{-1} \circ \varphi\) and
3914	  \(\psi \mapsto e \circ \psi\) are mutually inverse, since \(e \circ e^{-1} =
3915	  \mathrm{id}_S\) and \(e^{-1} \circ e = \mathrm{id}_R\) as ring homomorphisms, so the
3916	  two composites are the identity on functionals. Each assignment is \(R\)-linear:
3917	  additivity is immediate, and for a scalar \(r \in R\) the defining relation
3918	  \(r \cdot m = e(r) \cdot m\) of the pulled-back module structure makes each linearity
3919	  check a single rewrite --- \(e^{-1}\) (resp.\ \(e\)) intertwines the \(R\)-action on
3920	  the source with the \(R\)-action on the target precisely because \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\)
3921	  are inverse ring homomorphisms identifying \(R\) with \(S\). The forward map is
3922	  therefore an \(R\)-linear bijection, i.e.\ an \(R\)-linear equivalence; packaging the
3923	  two one-sided maps with the inverse identities gives
3924	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoDualEquiv}\).
3925	\end{proof}

3927	\begin{definition}
3928	
3929	\leanok
3930	[Precomposition equivalence on dual sections]
3931	  \label{def:presheaf_dual_precomp_equiv}
3932	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dualPrecompEquiv}
3933	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual}
3934	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings and let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\)
3935	  be an isomorphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. On a fixed open \(U\), precomposition
3936	  with \(e\) gives an \(R(U)\)-linear equivalence between the dual sections
3937	  \[
3938	    \mathtt{dualPrecompEquiv}\,e\,U \;\colon\;
3939	      \bigl(M'|_U \to R|_U\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \bigl(M|_U \to R|_U\bigr),
3940	      \qquad \varphi \;\longmapsto\; \varphi \circ e|_U,
3941	  \]
3942	  with inverse \(\psi \mapsto \psi \circ (e^{-1})|_U\). This is the section-level core of
3943	  the contravariant functoriality of the presheaf dual \(M \mapsto M^\vee\)
3944	  (\cref{def:presheaf_dual}) in isomorphisms: precomposing a functional on \(M'\) with
3945	  the comparison isomorphism produces a functional on \(M\), \(R(U)\)-linearly and
3946	  invertibly.

3949	\begin{definition}

3961	  --- note the reversal of direction, the dual being contravariant --- assembled
3962	  open-by-open from the precomposition equivalences
3963	  \(\mathtt{dualPrecompEquiv}\,e\,U\) of \cref{def:presheaf_dual_precomp_equiv}.
3964	  Naturality in \(U\), i.e.\ compatibility of these sectionwise equivalences with

3969	\begin{definition}
3970	
3971	
3972	\leanok
3973	[The sheaf-level dual carries an iso \(M \cong M'\) to an iso \(\mathtt{dual}\,M' \cong \mathtt{dual}\,M\)]
3974	  \label{def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso}
3975	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dualIsoOfIso}

3977	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) an isomorphism in

3979	  duals
3980	  \[

3984	  obtained by sheafifying the presheaf isomorphism \(\mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\,e\) of

3991	
3992	\begin{lemma}
3993	
3994	\leanok
3995	[Open-immersion slice-site sheaf equivalence (the shared slice bridge)]
3996	  \label{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
3997	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overSliceSheafEquiv}
3998	  % SOURCE: Mathlib code-location provenance (this is NOT an external literature
3999	  % citation, so it carries no verbatim mathematical SOURCE QUOTE). This lemma
4000	  % completes the documented TODO at Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean, lines
4001	  % 19--22, reproduced here for orientation:
4002	  %   "show that both functors of the equivalence overEquivalence U are continuous
4003	  %    and induce an equivalence between Sheaf ((Opens.grothendieckTopology X).over U) A
4004	  %    and Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology U) A for any category A."
4005	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subset, with associated open
4006	  immersion \(\iota : U \hookrightarrow X\). The reindexing equivalence
4007	  \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U : \mathtt{Over}\,U \simeq \mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\)
4008	  --- between the slice of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) over \(U\) and the opens of the subspace
4009	  \(\widetilde{U}\) --- upgrades to an \emph{equivalence of sheaf categories}
4010	  \[
4011	    \mathtt{Sheaf}\bigl((\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,X).\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr)\,A
4012	      \;\;\simeq\;\;
4013	    \mathtt{Sheaf}\bigl(\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,U\bigr)\,A
4014	  \]
4015	  for any target category \(A\), compatible with the module-pullback comparison
4016	  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). This completes the Mathlib TODO recorded at
4017	  \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} (lines 19--22). It is the \emph{shared root}
4018	  underlying both the restriction-compatibility isomorphism for the dual
4019	  (\cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}) and the morphism-descent / gluing engine
4020	  (\cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}): both reduce to identifying a
4021	  slice-indexed family of sections over \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\) with an
4022	  open-immersion-indexed family over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\), and that
4023	  identification is exactly this equivalence.
4024	\end{lemma}
4025	
4026	\begin{proof}
4027	
4028	\leanok
4029	  The functor \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U\) realises \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\) as a
4030	  \emph{dense subsite} of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\): every open of the subspace
4031	  \(\widetilde{U}\) is covered by down-sets \(\{V : V \le U\}\), and these down-sets are
4032	  precisely the objects of the slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\). One therefore applies the
4033	  dense-subsite transfer \(\mathtt{Functor.IsDenseSubsite.sheafEquiv}\): a continuous,
4034	  cocontinuous, dense inclusion of sites induces an equivalence of the associated sheaf
4035	  categories. The continuity and cover-lifting hypotheses are supplied by the slice-site
4036	  instances of \(\mathtt{Sites/Over}\) (the Grothendieck topology
4037	  \(J.\mathtt{over}\,U\) and its comparison with the topology pulled back along
4038	  \(\iota\)).
4039	
4040	  The over-category coherence that is laborious for a general site --- the pseudofunctor
4041	  identities \(\mathtt{Over.mapId}\), \(\mathtt{Over.mapComp}\) and the associativity
4042	  \(\mathtt{overMapPullback\_assoc}\) --- is here \emph{automatic}: because
4043	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) is a thin poset, every hom-set is a subsingleton, so each such
4044	  square commutes by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) (and its heterogeneous form
4045	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.helim}\) where source and target objects differ only up to a
4046	  propositional equality of opens). The sole sectionwise content is the down-set
4047	  identification
4048	  \[
4049	    \iota_{!}\bigl(\iota^{-1}(V)\bigr) \;=\; V
4050	    \qquad (V \le U),
4051	  \]
4052	  i.e.\ the image under the open immersion of the preimage of an open contained in \(U\)
4053	  is that open again. Transporting sections along this equality and invoking the
4054	  dense-subsite equivalence yields the stated sheaf equivalence, compatible with
4055	  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) by the same thinness argument.
4056	\end{proof}

4058	\begin{lemma}
4059	[Restriction commutes with the dual along an open immersion (the C-bridge)]
4060	  \label{lem:dual_restrict_iso}
4061	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_restrict_iso}
4062	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
4063	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom,
4064	  % Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM)
4065	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3710--L3721).
4066	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4067	  % "Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a morphism
4068	  % of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules.
4069	  % If $\mathcal{F}$ is finitely presented and $f$ is flat,
4070	  % then the canonical map
4071	  % $$
4072	  % f^*\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
4073	  % \longrightarrow
4074	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(f^*\mathcal{F}, f^*\mathcal{G})
4075	  % $$
4076	  % is an isomorphism."
4077	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom,
4078	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM).}
4079	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes, with image the open
4080	  \(U \subseteq X\), and let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a
4081	  canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
4082	  \[
4083	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)\big|_f \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(M|_f\bigr),
4084	  \]
4085	  natural in \(M\), between the restriction of the sheaf-level dual
4086	  (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}) and the dual of the restriction. (Only opens
4087	  \(V \subseteq U\) intervene downstream, where the comparison is needed.)
4088	\end{lemma}
4089	
4090	\begin{proof}
4091	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
4092	  This is the classical internal-hom base-change isomorphism
4093	  \(f^*\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X) \cong
4094	  \mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(f^*M, f^*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
4095	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom}, specialised to the structure sheaf
4096	  \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{O}_X\). For an open immersion the morphism \(f\) is flat and
4097	  restriction \((-)|_f\) is the exact pullback \(f^*\), so the finite-presentation and
4098	  flatness hypotheses of the general ringed-space statement are automatic; this is the
4099	  \emph{favorable} (open-immersion) case of the base-change comparison, in which the map
4100	  is an isomorphism for \emph{every} \(M\), not only the finitely-presented ones.
4101	
4102	  We emphasise at the outset that this is a genuine \emph{natural isomorphism}, not a
4103	  definitional equality: the two sides are not equal on the nose --- they carry
4104	  \emph{different module structures} that a sectionwise ring isomorphism must reconcile
4105	  --- so the comparison must be constructed and its naturality verified, even though the
4106	  underlying additive groups of functionals coincide. The construction lives at the
4107	  \emph{presheaf-of-modules level}, where the residual genuinely sits, and is carried out
4108	  objectwise in the open \(V\) (with \(V \subseteq U\)) from three ingredients.
4109	
4110	  \emph{(a) The per-\(V\) slice equivalence.} Because \(f\) is an open immersion, its
4111	  direct-image-of-opens functor \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) is fully faithful with image
4112	  the down-set \(\{W : W \le U\}\); consequently, for each open \(V \subseteq U\) there is
4113	  an equivalence of slice categories
4114	  \[
4115	    \mathtt{Over}_Y\,V \;\simeq\; \mathtt{Over}_X\bigl(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\,V\bigr),
4116	  \]
4117	  the per-open shadow of the reindexing equivalence
4118	  \(\mathtt{TopologicalSpace.Opens.overEquivalence}\). This identifies the indexing slice
4119	  over which the internal hom on \(Y\) computes its value at \(V\) with the corresponding
4120	  slice over \(X\). This is a per-open, presheaf-level instrument, working one open \(V\)
4121	  at a time and respecting the varying-ring \(\mathcal{O}(V)\)-module structure that the
4122	  dual carries; the comparison is built entirely at this presheaf-of-modules level, where
4123	  the residual genuinely lives.
4124	
4125	  \emph{(b) Agreement of the codomain.} The codomain of the internal hom on both sides is
4126	  the structure sheaf. On opens \(V \subseteq U\) the structure sheaf of \(Y\) agrees with
4127	  that of \(X\) under the identification of (a) --- \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V) =
4128	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV)\) up to the canonical comparison --- so the target side of the hom
4129	  matches under the slice reindexing.
4130	
4131	  \emph{(c) The ring-iso transport of the module structure.} The only remaining
4132	  discrepancy is the module structure. The left-hand side
4133	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f\) carries, at \(V\), the \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-module structure;
4134	  the right-hand side \(\mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\) carries the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module
4135	  structure. The sectionwise comparison
4136	  \(\beta_V = (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathtt{hom}_V \colon
4137	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_Y(V)\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}, so
4138	  restriction of scalars along \(\beta_V\) is an \emph{equivalence}
4139	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isEquivalence\_of\_ringEquiv}\)). Its ModuleCat-level shadow
4140	  on duals is exactly \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}: it carries the
4141	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-linear dual onto the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear dual,
4142	  \(R(V)\)-linearly and invertibly.
4143	
4144	  Composing (a), (b) and (c) gives, for each \(V \subseteq U\), an
4145	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism between the two values, and one checks
4146	  naturality in \(V\). The slice-reindexing coherence (a) is \emph{light}, because
4147	  \(\mathrm{Opens}\) is a thin poset and all the relevant squares commute by
4148	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\); but the module-structure transport (c) does
4149	  \emph{not} trivialise --- it is the substantive content, the place where the two sides
4150	  differ by a real ring isomorphism rather than an identity. Assembling the sectionwise
4151	  isomorphisms into a presheaf-of-modules natural isomorphism, and sheafifying through the
4152	  sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}, yields the stated isomorphism
4153	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f \cong \mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\).
4154	\end{proof}
4155	
4156	\begin{lemma}
4157	[The dual of a line bundle is a line bundle]
4158	  \label{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}
4159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_isLocallyTrivial}
4160	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:dual_restrict_iso,
4161	        def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
4162	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
4163	  % modules), Lemma~lemma-constructions-invertible (item~2)
4164	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200--L4213).
4165	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4166	  % "\item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
4167	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
4168	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
4169	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
4170	  % is an isomorphism."
4171	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR,
4172	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (item~2).}
4173	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with
4174	  \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\). Then the sheaf-level dual
4175	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} is again locally trivial of
4176	  rank one, i.e.\ \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,L)\).
4177	\end{lemma}
4178	
4179	\begin{proof}
4180	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:dual_restrict_iso,
4181	        def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
4182	  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an affine open \(U\) with a
4183	  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\); writing \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) for the
4184	  associated open immersion, it suffices to exhibit an isomorphism
4185	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). This is the following three-step chain:
4186	  \[
4187	    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)\big|_f
4188	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_f\bigr)
4189	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U
4190	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_U.
4191	  \]
4192	  \begin{enumerate}
4193	    \item The first isomorphism is the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
4194	      \cref{lem:dual_restrict_iso}: restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes
4195	      with the formation of the dual. This is the C-bridge proper; its substantive content
4196	      is the sectionwise ring-iso transport of the module structure
4197	      (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}), as detailed there.
4198	    \item The second isomorphism is the dual applied to the trivialisation
4199	      \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\), via the contravariant functoriality of the sheaf-level
4200	      dual in isomorphisms (\(\mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\),
4201	      \cref{def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso}): an isomorphism \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\)
4202	      induces \(\mathtt{dual}(L|_U) \cong \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U\).
4203	    \item The third isomorphism trivialises the dual of the unit,
4204	      \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (\(\mathtt{dual\_unit\_iso}\)):
4205	      the dual of the structure sheaf is the structure sheaf itself, the comparison being
4206	      evaluation at \(1 \in \mathcal{O}_U\), under which
4207	      \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U) \cong \mathcal{O}_U\).
4208	  \end{enumerate}
4209	  Composing the three exhibits \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). Hence
4210	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) is locally trivial of rank one, as required by item~2 of Stacks
4211	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (tag 01CR).
4212	\end{proof}

4214	\begin{lemma}
4215	
4216	
4217	\leanok
4218	[Local isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules glue to a global isomorphism]
4219	  \label{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}
4220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_restrict}
4221	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi \colon M \to N\) a morphism in
4222	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\).
4223	  Suppose that for every point \(x \in X\) there is an open neighbourhood \(U_x\) of
4224	  \(x\) such that the restriction of \(\varphi\) to \(U_x\) --- the image
4225	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) under the
4226	  restriction functor along the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\colon U_x \to X\) ---
4227	  is an isomorphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_x\). Then \(\varphi\) is an
4228	  isomorphism.
4229	\end{lemma}
4230	
4231	\begin{proof}
4232	  Being an isomorphism for a morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules can be
4233	  checked on the underlying morphism of sheaves of abelian groups, and there it can be
4234	  checked stalkwise. Fix a point \(x \in X\). Choose a preimage \(x' \in U_x\) of \(x\)
4235	  under the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\). By hypothesis the restriction
4236	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) is an
4237	  isomorphism, hence so is its image under any functor --- in particular under the
4238	  stalk-at-\(x'\) functor on the underlying ab-sheaves. The natural isomorphism
4239	  ``restriction commutes with stalks'' (\(\mathtt{restrictStalkNatIso}\)) identifies
4240	  this with the stalk of (the underlying ab-sheaf morphism of) \(\varphi\) at the image
4241	  point \(x\); transporting along it shows that stalk is an isomorphism. As this holds
4242	  at every \(x\), and a morphism of sheaves of abelian groups all of whose stalks are
4243	  isomorphisms is itself an isomorphism (stalkwise iso-detection,
4244	  \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_stalkFunctor\_map\_iso}\) on \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\)), the
4245	  underlying ab-sheaf morphism of \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism. Finally the forgetful
4246	  functor \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules.toPresheaf}\) reflects isomorphisms, so \(\varphi\)
4247	  itself is an isomorphism.
4248	\end{proof}
