4250	\begin{definition}
4251	
4252	
4253	
4254	\leanok
4255	[Scheme-level linearity wrapper \(\mathtt{homMk}\) (A-bridge step (ii))]
4256	  \label{def:scheme_modules_homMk}
4257	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homMk}
4258	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Given a morphism
4259	  \(g \colon M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf} \to N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}\) of
4260	  the underlying presheaves of abelian groups, together with a hypothesis \(hg\) that
4261	  \(g\) is \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear sectionwise, \(\mathtt{homMk}\,g\,hg\) is the
4262	  morphism \(M \to N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) that this data determines. It
4263	  is a thin wrapper that packages \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.homMk}\) at the scheme
4264	  level: the sectionwise-linearity datum promotes the ab-group morphism to a genuine
4265	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Its \(\mathtt{@[simp]}\) companion
4266	  \(\mathtt{toPresheaf\_map\_homMk}\) records that forgetting back recovers \(g\), i.e.\
4267	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,X).\mathtt{map}\,(\mathtt{homMk}\,g\,hg) = g\). This is step
4268	  (ii) of the A-bridge \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}: once the
4269	  underlying ab-sheaf morphism has been glued, \(\mathtt{homMk}\) re-attaches the
4270	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linearity to produce the global morphism of modules.
4271	\end{definition}
4272	
4273	\begin{lemma}
4274	[Compatible local morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules glue to a global morphism]
4275	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}
4276	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homOfLocalCompat}
4277	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and \(\{U_i\}\) an
4278	  open cover of \(X\). Suppose given, for each \(i\), a morphism
4279	  \(f_i \colon M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_i\) such that
4280	  for all \(i, j\) the restrictions of \(f_i\) and \(f_j\) to \(U_i \cap U_j\) agree.
4281	  Then there is a unique global morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
4282	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) whose restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\).
4283	\end{lemma}
4284	
4285	\begin{proof}
4286	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_homMk, lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
4287	  A morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is, concretely, a morphism of the
4288	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups that is in addition \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear
4289	  sectionwise. We build \(f\) in two steps.
4290	
4291	  \emph{(i) Glue the underlying ab-sheaf morphism.} Forget \(M\) and \(N\) to their
4292	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups. The morphisms-between-them presheaf
4293	  \(\mathcal{H} := \mathtt{presheafHom}\,(M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf})\,
4294	  (N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf})\), whose value at an open \(W\) is the abelian
4295	  group of morphisms \(M|_W \to N|_W\) of presheaves of abelian groups, is itself a
4296	  \emph{sheaf of types} --- this is the ``hom into a sheaf is a sheaf'' principle
4297	  \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsSheaf.hom}\), which consumes the sheaf condition of \(N\).
4298	  Regarding \(\mathcal{H}\) as a \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\), its universal gluing
4299	  property \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) amalgamates any compatible family of local
4300	  sections of \(\mathcal{H}\) into a unique global section. This route is preferred over
4301	  the raw sieve-level amalgamation \(\mathtt{presheafHom\_isSheafFor}\), which carries
4302	  strictly more bookkeeping.
4303	
4304	  The load-bearing datum is, for each \(i\), the \emph{local section}
4305	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i \in \mathcal{H}.\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathrm{op}\,U_i)\)
4306	  manufactured from \(f_i\). Its component at a pair \((V, h : V \to U_i)\) is the given
4307	  component of \(f_i\), conjugated by the canonical \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) identifications
4308	  that arise from the open-set equality \(\iota_i\bigl(\iota_i^{-1}(V)\bigr) = V\) valid
4309	  for \(V \subseteq U_i\) (the image under the open immersion \(\iota_i : U_i \to X\) of
4310	  the preimage of \(V\) is \(V\) again). The genuine coherence risk of the whole
4311	  construction is concentrated in the \emph{naturality field} of
4312	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\): the requirement that these
4313	  \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugated components of \(f_i\) commute across the morphisms of
4314	  the slice category \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\). This is the one sub-piece that should be
4315	  built and verified first, as a standalone axiom-clean lemma.
4316	
4317	  This \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality is precisely the \emph{section-direction
4318	  slice} of the comparison packaged in \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}:
4319	  converting the local datum \(f_i\), which lives over the open-immersion pullback
4320	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U_i}\), into a section of \(\mathcal{H}\) indexed over the
4321	  slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\) is the one-sided realisation of the open-immersion
4322	  \(\leftrightarrow\) slice transport. As there, the \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation is
4323	  controlled by the down-set identity \(\iota_i(\iota_i^{-1}(V)) = V\) for
4324	  \(V \subseteq U_i\), and the coherence is automatic on the thin poset
4325	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). This A-engine uses the
4326	  sheaf-site equivalence \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}; the C-bridge
4327	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}, by contrast, uses a per-open slice comparison at
4328	  the presheaf level over the \emph{varying} section ring \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), and so
4329	  does \emph{not} reduce to the same sheaf-site root --- the two are independent and
4330	  must be built separately.
4331	
4332	  The remaining four sub-steps are mechanical: (a) the
4333	  cocycle/\(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\) condition on the family
4334	  \((\mathtt{localSection}\,i)_i\), which is exactly the assumed agreement of \(f_i\)
4335	  and \(f_j\) on the overlaps \(U_i \cap U_j\); (b) gluing via
4336	  \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) and converting the amalgamated section over
4337	  \(\mathrm{op}\,\top\) into a genuine morphism \(g \colon M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}
4338	  \to N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}\) of the underlying presheaves --- the
4339	  \(\top\)-section-to-morphism passage being the global-sections equivalence
4340	  \(\mathtt{presheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) / \(\mathtt{sheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) of the
4341	  hom-sheaf \(\mathcal{H}\) (which, since \(\top\) is terminal in \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\),
4342	  lands the glued section as an honest morphism of presheaves), \emph{not} a hand-unfold
4343	  of the \(\top\)-section; (c) the
4344	  sectionwise linearity check; and (d) the recovery that the restriction of \(g\) to
4345	  each \(U_i\) is the ab-sheaf morphism underlying \(f_i\).
4346	
4347	  \emph{(ii) Promote to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear.} The linearity equation
4348	  \(g(r \cdot m) = r \cdot g(m)\) is a sectionwise identity between two sections of a
4349	  separated presheaf. It holds on each \(U_i\), because there \(g\) restricts to the
4350	  module morphism \(f_i\); since the two sides agree on the cover \(\{U_i\}\) and the
4351	  ambient presheaf is separated, they agree globally. Packaging \(g\) together with this
4352	  global linearity through the scheme-level wrapper \cref{def:scheme_modules_homMk}
4353	  (\(\mathtt{homMk}\)) yields a morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
4354	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); its restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\) and its
4355	  uniqueness are both inherited from the ab-sheaf amalgamation of step (i).
4356	
4357	  This gluing engine is substantially larger than a routine glue: it is dominated by the
4358	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality field and the convert-and-recover bookkeeping, and
4359	  the naturality is the section-direction slice of
4360	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} --- so its true cost is bounded below by
4361	  building that sheaf-site equivalence, not by the surrounding assembly. It is therefore to be
4362	  scoped as a multi-piece build --- with \(\mathtt{localSection}\), together with its
4363	  naturality, built as a standalone verified lemma first --- rather than a one-shot.
4364	  The step computes no tensor stalk --- it is purely a statement about gluing morphisms
4365	  of sheaves --- and so it does not re-enter the stalk-tensor commutation.
4366	\end{proof}
4367	
4368	\begin{remark}
4369	[How the dual infrastructure discharges the \(\otimes\)-inverse]
4370	  \label{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}
4371	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial,
4372	        lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat, lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict,
4373	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
4374	  Once \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom,def:presheaf_dual,%
4375	  lem:internal_hom_isSheaf,lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} are available,
4376	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is discharged with no further missing object:
4377	  take \(L^{-1} := \mathtt{dual}\,L\) (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}), which is a line
4378	  bundle by \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}. The global contraction \(\varepsilon_L
4379	  \colon L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{not} obtained by sheafifying
4380	  the presheaf-level evaluation; it is instead built by \emph{gluing the canonical local
4381	  contraction morphisms}. On each trivialising open \(U\), the
4382	  restriction-compatibility iso \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} carries
4383	  \((L \otimes_X L^{-1})|_U\) to \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U\), and the
4384	  canonical local contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\)
4385	  of the free rank-one module with its dual is the left unitor
4386	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, an isomorphism. These local contractions are canonical,
4387	  hence agree on overlaps, and so glue --- by the A-bridge
4388	  \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat} --- to a single global morphism
4389	  \(\varepsilon_L \colon L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\). Since ``being an
4390	  isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) and \(\varepsilon_L\) restricts to an isomorphism on
4391	  each \(U\), it is a global isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim}
4392	  \mathcal{O}_X\) by the B-connector \cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}.
4393	
4394	  This descent construction deliberately avoids the forbidden
4395	  ``sheafify-the-evaluation'' route --- which would re-enter the abandoned
4396	  tensor-stalk / sheafification-unit whiskering commutation --- and computes no tensor
4397	  stalk anywhere. It is the realization the ``infrastructure-blocked'' note in
4398	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} points to.
4399	\end{remark}
4400	
4401	\begin{remark}
4402	[Alternative route via object-level descent (fallback)]
4403	  \label{rem:dual_via_stack}
4404	  Should the slice-and-sheafify route of
4405	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom,lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} bottom out, the dual can
4406	  instead be assembled by \emph{object-level descent}: glue the local trivial duals
4407	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) along the inverse-transpose transition isomorphisms
4408	  \(g_{ij}^{-\mathsf{T}}\) of \(L\) into a global \(L^{-1}\) on \(X\). Abstractly this
4409	  is effective descent for sheaves of modules on the small Zariski site --- the
4410	  essential-surjectivity of the descent-data functor of an \(\mathtt{IsStack}\)
4411	  pseudofunctor \(U \mapsto (\mathcal{O}_U\text{-modules})\). This is the more
4412	  principled and reusable construction but the larger build (it requires constructing
4413	  the restriction pseudofunctor and proving the stack/effective-descent property),
4414	  and is recorded only as a fallback; the slice internal-hom route above is the
4415	  intended one. It is the same morphism-/object-level descent family on which the
4416	  associator re-route of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} also waits.
4417	\end{remark}

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tags 02KE
5	% (Quasi-coherence of higher direct images), 02KG (relative affine vanishing),
6	% and 02KH (Flat base change) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
7	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
8	
9	\section{Setup and motivation}
10	
11	This chapter treats the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
12	\(i \geq 1\), complementing the \(i = 0\) (direct-image) case of
13	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}. These higher direct images are the
14	single deepest foundation of the cohomology-and-base-change package: the
15	Cohen--Macaulay regularity bound, semicontinuity of fibre cohomology, and the
16	\(m\)-regularity estimate of FGA all rest on the basic finiteness and base-change
17	properties of \(R^i f_*\) recorded here.
18	
19	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category) and a
20	{\v C}ech-complex approach, parallel to the \(i = 0\) treatment of
21	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}.
22	
23	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
24	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
25	A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded by the
26	cartesian square obtained from the fibre product \(X' = X \times_S S'\):
27	\[
28	  \begin{array}{ccc}
29	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
30	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
31	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
32	  \end{array}
33	\]
34	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
35	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
36	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
37	
38	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
39	
40	\begin{definition}\leanok
41	[Higher direct image]
42	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
43	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
44	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
45	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
46	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
47	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
48	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
49	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
50	  % The file is not yet imported by the aggregator (orphan module).
51	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
52	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
53	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
54	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
55	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
57	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
58	  % the sheaf
59	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
60	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
61	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
62	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
63	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
64	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
65	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
66	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
67	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
68	  sheafification of the presheaf
69	  \[
70	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
71	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
72	  \]
73	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
74	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
75	\end{definition}
76	
77	\section{Quasi-coherence of the higher direct images}
78	
79	\begin{lemma}\leanok
80	[Quasi-coherence of higher direct images]
81	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent}
82	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_isQuasiCoherent}
83	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
84	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KE (Lemma
85	  % `lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images', part (1))
86	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L741--757).
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
88	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
89	  % \begin{enumerate}
90	  % \item For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$ the
91	  % higher direct images $R^pf_*\mathcal{F}$ are quasi-coherent on $S$.
92	  % \item If $S$ is quasi-compact, there exists an integer $n = n(X, S, f)$
93	  % such that $R^pf_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $p \geq n$ and any
94	  % quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$.
95	  % \item In fact, if $S$ is quasi-compact we can find $n = n(X, S, f)$
96	  % such that for every
97	  % morphism of schemes $S' \to S$ we have $R^p(f')_*\mathcal{F}' = 0$
98	  % for $p \geq n$ and any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}'$
99	  % on $X'$. Here $f' : X' = S' \times_S X \to S'$ is the base change of $f$.
100	  % \end{enumerate}"
101	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KE (``Quasi-coherence of higher direct
102	  images''), part (1).}
103	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated morphism of schemes.
104	  Then for every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) and every
105	  \(p \geq 0\), the higher direct image \(R^p f_*\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent
106	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module.
107	\end{lemma}
108	
109	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We first prove (1). Note that under the hypotheses of the
110	% lemma the sheaf
111	% $R^0f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent by
112	% Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-push-forward-quasi-coherent}.
113	% Using
114	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-localize-higher-direct-images}
115	% we see that forming higher direct images commutes with restriction
116	% to open subschemes. Since being quasi-coherent is local on $S$ we
117	% reduce to the case discussed in the next paragraph.
118	%
119	% Proof of (1) in case $S$ is affine. We will use the induction principle.
120	% Since $f$ quasi-compact and quasi-separated we see that $X$
121	% is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. For $U \subset X$
122	% quasi-compact open and $a = f|_U$ we let $P(U)$ be the property that
123	% $R^pa_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent on $S$ for all quasi-coherent modules
124	% $\mathcal{F}$ on $U$ and all $p \geq 0$. Since $P(X)$ is (1), it suffices
125	% the prove conditions (1) and (2) of Lemma \ref{lemma-induction-principle} hold.
126	% If $U$ is affine, then $P(U)$ holds because $R^pa_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for
127	% $p \geq 1$ (by  Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} and
128	% Morphisms, Lemma \ref{morphisms-lemma-morphism-affines-affine})
129	% and we've already observed the result holds for $p = 0$ in the first
130	% paragraph. Next, let $U \subset X$ be a quasi-compact open, $V \subset X$
131	% an affine open, and assume $P(U)$, $P(V)$, $P(U \cap V)$ hold.
132	% Let $a = f|_U$, $b = f|_V$, $c = f|_{U \cap V}$, and $g = f|_{U \cup V}$.
133	% Then for any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_{U \cup V}$-module $\mathcal{F}$
134	% we have the relative Mayer-Vietoris sequence
135	% $$
136	% 0 \to
137	% g_*\mathcal{F} \to
138	% a_*(\mathcal{F}|_U) \oplus b_*(\mathcal{F}|_V) \to
139	% c_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U \cap V}) \to
140	% R^1g_*\mathcal{F} \to \ldots
141	% $$
142	% see Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-relative-mayer-vietoris}.
143	% By $P(U)$, $P(V)$, $P(U \cap V)$ we see that
144	% $R^pa_*(\mathcal{F}|_U)$, $R^pb_*(\mathcal{F}|_V)$ and
145	% $R^pc_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U \cap V})$ are all quasi-coherent.
146	% Using the results on quasi-coherent sheaves in
147	% Schemes, Section \ref{schemes-section-quasi-coherent}
148	% this implies that each of the sheaves
149	% $R^pg_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent since it sits in the middle of a short
150	% exact sequence with a cokernel of a map between quasi-coherent sheaves
151	% on the left and a kernel of a map between quasi-coherent sheaves on the right.
152	% Whence $P(U \cup V)$ and the proof of (1) is complete."
153	\begin{proof}
154	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}
155	  Quasi-coherence is local on \(S\), and forming higher direct images commutes
156	  with restriction to open subschemes, so we may assume \(S\) is affine; then
157	  \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. We argue by the induction principle
158	  for quasi-compact opens of a quasi-compact quasi-separated scheme: for a
159	  quasi-compact open \(U \subset X\) with \(a = f|_U\), let \(P(U)\) be the
160	  property that \(R^p a_*\mathcal{G}\) is quasi-coherent on \(S\) for every
161	  quasi-coherent module \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(U\) and every \(p \geq 0\); the
162	  desired statement is \(P(X)\).
163	
164	  \emph{Affine base.} If \(U\) is affine then \(a\) is an affine morphism, so
165	  \(R^p a_*\mathcal{G} = 0\) for \(p \geq 1\) by the relative affine vanishing of
166	  Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}, while
167	  \(R^0 a_*\mathcal{G} = a_*\mathcal{G}\) is quasi-coherent because the pushforward
168	  of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated morphism is
169	  quasi-coherent. Hence \(P(U)\) holds.
170	
171	  \emph{Inductive step.} Let \(U \subset X\) be quasi-compact open, \(V \subset X\)
172	  affine open, and suppose \(P(U)\), \(P(V)\), \(P(U \cap V)\) hold. Writing
173	  \(a = f|_U\), \(b = f|_V\), \(c = f|_{U \cap V}\), \(h = f|_{U \cup V}\), the
174	  relative Mayer--Vietoris sequence
175	  \[
176	    0 \to h_*\mathcal{G} \to a_*(\mathcal{G}|_U) \oplus b_*(\mathcal{G}|_V)
177	      \to c_*(\mathcal{G}|_{U \cap V}) \to R^1 h_*\mathcal{G} \to \cdots
178	  \]
179	  exhibits each \(R^p h_*\mathcal{G}\) as an extension built from quasi-coherent
180	  sheaves \(R^p a_*(\mathcal{G}|_U)\), \(R^p b_*(\mathcal{G}|_V)\),
181	  \(R^p c_*(\mathcal{G}|_{U \cap V})\): it sits between the cokernel of a map of
182	  quasi-coherent sheaves and the kernel of a map of quasi-coherent sheaves, hence
183	  is quasi-coherent. Thus \(P(U \cup V)\) holds. By the induction principle
184	  \(P(X)\) holds, proving the lemma.
185	\end{proof}
186	
187	\section{Vanishing of higher direct images along an affine morphism}
188	
189	\begin{lemma}\leanok
190	[Relative affine vanishing]
191	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}
192	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_affine_eq_zero}
193	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
194	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KG (Lemma
195	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing')
196	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L180--185).
197	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
198	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
199	  % If $f$ is affine then $R^if_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $i > 0$."
200	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KG (``Relative affine vanishing'').}
201	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) a
202	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. If \(f\) is affine, then
203	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F} = 0\) for all \(i \geq 1\).
204	\end{lemma}
205	
206	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
207	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
208	% the sheaf
209	% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
210	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
211	% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
212	% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
213	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
214	% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
215	% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
216	% opens we win."
217	\begin{proof}
218	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
219	  By Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}, \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the
220	  sheafification of the presheaf \(V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V),
221	  \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\). Since \(f\) is affine, the preimage \(f^{-1}(V)\) of
222	  any affine open \(V \subset S\) is affine. The higher cohomology of a
223	  quasi-coherent sheaf on an affine scheme vanishes, so
224	  \(H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for all \(i \geq 1\) and all
225	  affine \(V\). As the affine opens form a basis of \(S\), the sheafification of a
226	  presheaf that vanishes on a basis is zero; hence \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F} = 0\) for
227	  all \(i \geq 1\).
228	\end{proof}
229	
230	\section{Flat base change for the higher direct images}
231	
232	\begin{theorem}\leanok
233	[Flat base change, $i \geq 1$ case]
234	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_higher}
235	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_higherDirectImage_isIso}
236	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent,
237	    def:pushforward_base_change_map}
238	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (Lemma "Flat base change"), the
239	  % $i \geq 1$ case (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970, and
240	  % references/stacks-coherent.md).
241	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
242	  % $$
243	  % \xymatrix{
244	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
245	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
246	  % }
247	  % $$
248	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
249	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
250	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
251	  % For any $i \geq 0$
252	  % \begin{enumerate}
253	  % \item the base change map of
254	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
255	  % is an isomorphism
256	  % $$
257	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
258	  % $$
259	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
260	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
261	  % \end{enumerate}"
262	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
263	  \(i \geq 1\) case.}
264	  Consider the cartesian square
265	  \[
266	    \begin{array}{ccc}
267	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
268	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
269	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
270	    \end{array}
271	  \]
272	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
273	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\) is
274	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then for every \(i \geq 1\) the canonical
275	  base-change map (the higher-degree analogue of the pushforward base-change map
276	  of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is an isomorphism
277	  \[
278	    g^*\bigl(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
279	    R^i f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
280	  \]
281	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
282	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map of
283	  \(B\)-modules
284	  \[
285	    H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
286	    H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
287	  \]
288	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
289	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
290	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
291	\end{theorem}
292	
293	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Having said this, part (1) follows from part (2). Namely,
294	% part (1) is local on $S'$ and hence we may assume $S$
295	% and $S'$ are affine. In other words, we have $S = \Spec(A)$
296	% and $S' = \Spec(B)$ as in (2).
297	% Then since $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent
298	% (Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}),
299	% it is the quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_S$-module associated to the
300	% $A$-module $H^0(S, R^if_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})$
301	% (equality by
302	% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}).
303	% Similarly, $R^if'_*\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent
304	% $\mathcal{O}_{S'}$-module associated to the $B$-module
305	% $H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$. Since pullback by $g$ corresponds
306	% to $- \otimes_A B$ on modules
307	% (Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback})
308	% we see that it suffices to prove (2).
309	%
310	% Let $A \to B$ be a flat ring homomorphism.

335	\begin{proof}
336	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent,
337	    def:pushforward_base_change_map}
338	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The assertion is local on \(S'\), so we
339	  may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with
340	  \(A \to B\) flat. By quasi-coherence of the higher direct images
341	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent}), \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is
342	  the quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module associated to the \(A\)-module
343	  \(H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})\), and likewise
344	  \(R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is associated to the \(B\)-module
345	  \(H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\). Since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
346	  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map is identified with the
347	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
348	  \[
349	    H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
350	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
351	  \]
352	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
353	
354	  \medskip
355	  \emph{The separated case via {\v C}ech complexes.} Suppose first that \(X\) is
356	  separated over \(A\). Choose a finite affine open cover
357	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\); by separatedness every finite
358	  intersection \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) is again affine, so {\v C}ech cohomology
359	  computes sheaf cohomology:
360	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\) for all \(p\).
361	  Base changing the cover yields an affine open cover
362	  \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of the again-separated
363	  \(X_B\), with the same property
364	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
365	  affine case identifies each term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the
366	  original term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\); that is, there is an isomorphism of
367	  cochain complexes
368	  \[
369	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \;\cong\;
370	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B .
371	  \]
372	  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, the functor \(- \otimes_A B\) is exact and hence
373	  commutes with the formation of cohomology of a complex; taking \(H^i\) in any
374	  degree \(i \geq 1\) gives the desired isomorphism
375	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\).
376	
377	  \medskip
378	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated.
379	  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\).
380	  Now the intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are quasi-compact and separated rather
381	  than affine, but they fall under the separated case already treated, so flat base
382	  change holds for each of them. Comparing the two {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
383	  sequences with \(E_2\)-pages
384	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\) and
385	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B))\), converging to
386	  \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\) and \(H^{p+q}(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) respectively, the
387	  separated case provides the term-by-term identification
388	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B)) =
389	  \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})) \otimes_A B\) on the
390	  \(E_2\)-pages; flatness of \(A \to B\) propagates this isomorphism through the
391	  spectral sequences to the abutment, giving
392	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) in every degree
393	  \(i \geq 1\). Translating back to sheaves on \(S'\) yields the asserted
394	  isomorphism \(g^*(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\).
395	\end{proof}

2564	\section{Pullback-monoidality: invertibility under a general scheme morphism}
2565	\label{sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality}
2566	
2567	The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) re-bases its
2568	line-bundle carrier onto the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2569	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and its
2570	functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the
2571	projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the base-change
2572	pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to descend to the
2573	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrier one needs that \emph{pullback preserves
2574	\(\otimes\)-invertibility} for \emph{general} morphisms of schemes --- the
2575	projection and the base-change morphism are neither open immersions nor flat.
2576	
2577	This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
2578	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
2579	restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
2580	\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
2581	strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
2582	map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). A general scheme
2583	morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable. The
2584	general case instead uses the \emph{left} adjoint --- pullback \(=\) inverse image
2585	\(=\) extension of scalars --- which is strong monoidal for \emph{any} ring map,
2586	with no iso hypothesis: extension of scalars \(- \otimes_R S\) along \(R \to S\)
2587	carries the tensorator \((M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S \cong (M \otimes_R S)
2588	\otimes_S (N \otimes_R S)\) unconditionally. This is the whole reason the general
2589	lemma is a separate result.
2590	
2591	\begin{lemma}
2592	[Pullback commutes with $\otimes$ for a general scheme morphism]
2593	  \label{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}
2594	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackTensorIso}
2595	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2596	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
2597	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
2598	  Writing \(f^* = \mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f\) for the inverse-image functor
2599	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules, the canonical comparison
2600	  \[
2601	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
2602	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2603	    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)
2604	  \]
2605	  is an isomorphism, natural in \(M\) and \(N\). No local-freeness, flatness, or
2606	  open-immersion hypothesis is required: it holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
2607	  and all morphisms \(f\).
2608	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
2609	  % \ref{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} (pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a
2610	  % morphism of ringed spaces commutes with tensor product, functorially)
2611	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2392-2400).
2612	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2613	  % "\begin{lemma}
2614	  % \label{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}
2615	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be
2616	  % a morphism of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$
2617	  % be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules. Then
2618	  % $f^*(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_Y} \mathcal{G})
2619	  % = f^*\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} f^*\mathcal{G}$
2620	  % functorially in $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$.
2621	  % \end{lemma}"
2622	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
2623	  \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}: for a morphism of ringed spaces
2624	  \(f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)\) and \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
2625	  \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\), one has \(f^*(\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) =
2626	  f^*\mathcal{F} \otimes f^*\mathcal{G}\) functorially.}
2627	\end{lemma}
2628	
2629	\begin{proof}
2630	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2631	  The proof mirrors the structure of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} but routes
2632	  through the \emph{left} adjoint, where no isomorphism hypothesis on the ring map
2633	  is needed.
2634	
2635	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce to the presheaf level).} By
2636	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}, the substrate tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
2637	  sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
2638	  \(M.\mathrm{val} \otimes^{p} N.\mathrm{val}\), where
2639	  \(\otimes^{p} = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\). The pullback
2640	  commutes with sheafification: the genuine Mathlib lemma
2641	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\) --- which is general
2642	  (it holds for any morphism of ring-sheaves, and is already consumed in the
2643	  open-immersion case \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) --- supplies a natural
2644	  isomorphism moving \(f^*\) inside the sheafification. The claim therefore reduces
2645	  to the presheaf-level comparison
2646	  \[
2647	    (\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,(A \otimes^{p} B)
2648	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2649	    \bigl((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A\bigr)
2650	      \;\otimes^{p}\;
2651	    \bigl((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B\bigr),
2652	  \]
2653	  where \(\varphi\) is the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of rings
2654	  induced by \(f\), and \(A = M.\mathrm{val}\), \(B = N.\mathrm{val}\).
2655	
2656	  \emph{Step 2 (the presheaf pullback is strong monoidal).} The presheaf-level
2657	  pullback \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) is, sectionwise,
2658	  \emph{extension of scalars} along the section ring maps of \(\varphi\) --- the
2659	  left adjoint of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\). Mathlib equips extension of scalars
2660	  along \emph{any} ring homomorphism \(\psi\) with a strong-monoidal structure,
2661	  \((\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\psi).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
2662	  (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean}); its
2663	  tensorator is the canonical isomorphism
2664	  \((M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S \cong (M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S (N \otimes_R S)\),
2665	  valid with \emph{no} hypothesis on \(\psi\). Assembling these sectionwise
2666	  tensorators against the presheaf restriction morphisms furnishes a strong-monoidal
2667	  structure on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), whose monoidal
2668	  comparison \(\mu\) is precisely the presheaf-level isomorphism required by Step~1.
2669	  Sheafifying \(\mu\) and transporting along the
2670	  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\) isomorphism of Step~1 yields the asserted
2671	  isomorphism \(f^*(M \otimes_X N) \cong (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)\). Naturality in
2672	  \(M, N\) is inherited from the naturality of \(\mu\) and of sheafification.
2673	
2674	  The contrast with \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is exactly the adjoint used:
2675	  there the comparison is produced from the \emph{right} adjoint
2676	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is strong monoidal only
2677	  because the open immersion makes the comparison ring map a (local) isomorphism;
2678	  here the \emph{left} adjoint \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\) is strong monoidal for
2679	  every ring map, so the hypothesis of an isomorphism is dropped entirely.
2680	\end{proof}

2705	  \((\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\psi).\mathtt{Monoidal}\): for a ring map
2706	  \(\psi : R \to S\) it is the canonical isomorphism \(R \otimes_R S \cong S\)
2707	  exhibiting that extension of scalars carries the base ring \(R\) (the
2708	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) unit over \(R\)) to \(S\) (the unit over \(S\)). Assembling
2709	  these sectionwise unit comparisons across the presheaf and sheafifying descends to
2710	  the asserted isomorphism \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\).

2788	\section{The invertibility-carrier Picard group}
2789	\label{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}
2790	
2791	The group law of \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} is carried on the
2792	\emph{locally-trivial} predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), and on that carrier
2793	the existence of a tensor inverse for each class is an extra obligation
2794	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}): one must \emph{manufacture} the dual
2795	object \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) and prove the
2796	contraction \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), which is the whole content of
2797	the dual-infrastructure build of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
2798	
2799	This section carries the group law on a \emph{different} predicate for which that
2800	obligation evaporates: the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2801	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
2802	\[
2803	  \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
2804	  \;:\equiv\;
2805	  \exists\, N,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\bigr).
2806	\]
2807	On this carrier the inverse of a class \([M]\) is supplied by the \emph{membership
2808	witness itself} --- the very \(N\) whose existence \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
2809	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
2810	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
2811	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
2812	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
2813	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
2814	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible
2815	modules (Stacks tag 01CX).
2816	
2817	\begin{lemma}
2818	
2819	\leanok
2820	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on invertible objects (corollary of the unconditional
2821	associator)]
2822	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2823	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2824	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2825	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
2826	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
2827	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
2828	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
2829	  \[
2830	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2831	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2832	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
2833	  \]
2834	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The invertibility-carrier group law
2835	  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
2836	  isomorphism, the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2837	  \cong M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality
2838	  coherence is ever required.

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).

37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}

39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''

50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87

89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}

94	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
95	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
96	  % "In Situation REF.
97	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
98	  %  \(\)
99	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
100	  %  \(\)
101	  %  with the following properties:
102	  %  \begin{enumerate}
103	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
104	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
105	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
106	  %  \(\)
107	  %  \xymatrix{
108	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
109	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
111	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
112	  %  }
113	  %  \(\)
114	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
115	  %  \end{enumerate}
116	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
117	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
118	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
119	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
120	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
121	  (Definition~REF). There exists a scheme
122	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
123	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
124	  \begin{enumerate}
125	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
126	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
127	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
128	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
129	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
130	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
131	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
132	  \end{enumerate}
133	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
134	  over \(X\).
135	\end{theorem}

137	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
138	% Lemmas REF,
139	% REF, and
140	% REF.
141	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
142	% Lemma REF."
143	\begin{proof}
144	  
145	  
146	\leanok
147	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
148	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
149	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
150	  open-immersion transition data
151	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
152	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
153	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
154	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
155	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
156	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
157	\end{proof}

365	  Let \(g : T \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a quasi-coherent

381	  
382	  \leanok

384	  % closed BOTH iter-179 Mathlib-gap sorries kernel-clean:
385	  % (i) ``Scheme.QcohAlgebra.pullback_coequifibered`` (the ``coequifibered``
386	  % field, RelativeSpec.lean L358-377) via

398	  % is functionally complete.
399	  By Theorem~REF, \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) represents
400	  the functor \(F\) sending an \(X\)-scheme \(h : S \to X\) to the set of
401	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra maps \(h^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_S\). A morphism
402	  \(S \to T \times_X \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) over \(T\) is the same as a pair of an
403	  \(X\)-morphism \(S \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with a \(T\)-factorisation
404	  of \(S \to X\), i.e.\ a morphism \(S \to T\) whose composition with \(g\) equals
405	  \(S \to X\). Tracing through, this is a \(T\)-morphism \(f' : S \to T\) together with an
406	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra map \((f')^*(g^*\mathcal{A}) = f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_S\).
407	  This is precisely \(F'(S)\), the functor for \((T, g^*\mathcal{A})\), so the canonical map
408	  \(T \times_X \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to \underline{\Spec}_T(g^*\mathcal{A})\)
409	  is an isomorphism by Yoneda.

412	\section{Functoriality}
413	\label{sec:relspec_functoriality}

415	\begin{theorem}\leanok
416	[Relative spectrum as a functor]
417	  \label{thm:relative_spec_functorial}
418	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.functor}
419	  
420	  % NOTE (iter-173 review): the iter-173 file-skeleton encodes this as a
421	  % bare object-level function ``QcohAlgebra → Over X'' (not a categorical
422	  % ``Functor''), with morphism action + functoriality + the equivalence
423	  % onto affine X-schemes all deferred. Iter-174+ upgrades to the full
424	  % contravariant ``Functor (X.QcohAlgebra)ᵒᵖ (Over X)'' with the universal
425	  % property-induced ``map'' once QcohAlgebra is unpacked.
426	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LR (definition-relative-spec +
427	  % lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec + lemma-spec-properties part (3))
428	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L602-L691).
429	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
430	  % "Let \(S\) be a scheme. Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a quasi-coherent sheaf of
431	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras. The {\it relative spectrum of \(\mathcal{A}\) over
432	  %  \(S\)}, or simply the {\it spectrum of \(\mathcal{A}\) over \(S\)} is the scheme
433	  %  constructed in Lemma REF which represents the
434	  %  functor \(F\) (REF), see
435	  %  Lemma REF.
436	  %  We denote it \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \to S\).
437	  %  The ``universal family'' is a morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras
438	  %  \(\)
439	  %  \mathcal{A}
440	  %  \longrightarrow
441	  %  \pi_*\mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})}
442	  %  \(\)
443	  %  [...]
444	  %  The universal map
445	  %  \(\)
446	  %  \mathcal{A}
447	  %  \longrightarrow
448	  %  \pi_*\mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})}
449	  %  \(\)
450	  %  is an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras."
451	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LR (definition-relative-spec +
452	  lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec + lemma-spec-properties);
453	  [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
454	  The construction \(\mathcal{A} \mapsto \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) assembles into
455	  a contravariant functor
456	  \[
457	    \underline{\Spec}_X : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathrm{AffSch}/X
458	  \]
459	  from quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras to affine \(X\)-schemes: a morphism of
460	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras \(\psi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) induces, by the universal
461	  property (Theorem~REF) applied to the pair
462	  $(\pi_\mathcal{B}, \psi \circ \pi_\mathcal{B}^*) :
463	  \pi_\mathcal{B}^*\mathcal{A} \to \pi_\mathcal{B}^*\mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec\mathcal{B}}$,
464	  an \(X\)-morphism
465	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\psi) : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\).
466	  Functoriality (identity, composition) is direct from the universal property. The
467	  pushforward \(\pi_* \mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})} \cong \mathcal{A}\)
468	  exhibits \(\underline{\Spec}_X\) as an equivalence between \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}\) and
469	  the full subcategory of affine \(X\)-schemes, with inverse \(f \mapsto f_* \mathcal{O}_Y\)
470	  (Stacks lemma-spec-properties part~(3)).
471	\end{theorem}
472	
473	\begin{proof}
474	  
475	  \leanok
476	  Functoriality follows directly from Theorem~REF: for
477	  \(\psi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\), the pair
478	  $(\pi_\mathcal{B} : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to X, \;
479	  \pi_\mathcal{B}^*(\psi))\( defines an \)X$-morphism
480	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) via the
481	  representing universal pair. Identity and composition follow from naturality of the
482	  representing bijection in \(\mathcal{A}\). For the equivalence claim, \(\underline{\Spec}_X\)
483	  is fully faithful because the isomorphism
484	  \(\pi_* \mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})} \cong \mathcal{A}\)
485	  (locally identifying with \(\Gamma(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)),\mathcal{O}) = \mathcal{A}(U)\)
486	  via Theorem~REF) reconstructs \(\mathcal{A}\) from
487	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\). Essential surjectivity onto affine \(X\)-schemes uses
488	  the canonical morphism \(X \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\pi_*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of Stacks
489	  lemma-canonical-morphism; on an affine \(X\)-scheme \(\pi : Y \to X\), this canonical
490	  morphism is an isomorphism (affine-locally it is the identity
491	  \(\Spec\Gamma(\pi^{-1}(U), \mathcal{O}_Y) \to \Spec\Gamma(\pi^{-1}(U), \mathcal{O}_Y)\)).
492	\end{proof}

494	\section{Lean encoding}
495	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
496	
497	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
498	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
499	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
500	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
501	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
502	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
503	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
504	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
505	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
506	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
507	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
508	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
509	(Theorem~REF) is expressed as a
510	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
511	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
512	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
513	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. The base-change isomorphism
514	(Theorem~REF) and functoriality
515	(Theorem~REF) are derived from the universal property by
516	Yoneda. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a definitional/structural
517	construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme infrastructure.
518	
519	\section{Out of scope}
520	\label{sec:relspec_out_of_scope}
521	
522	This chapter packages \emph{only} the relative-spectrum construction and its three core
523	properties. The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling chapters and
524	are explicitly out of scope here:
525	\begin{itemize}
526	  \item \textbf{Relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)} (A.1.c) --- consumed-by, not
527	    defined-here; its blueprint home is the planned chapter
528	    \texttt{Picard\_RelPicFunctor.tex}.
529	  \item \textbf{Line-bundle pullback on a product \(C \times_k T\)} (A.1.b) ---
530	    \texttt{Picard\_LineBundlePullback.tex} (planned).
531	  \item \textbf{Connectedness, dimensionality, smoothness of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)}
532	    --- downstream properties that depend on assumptions on \(\mathcal{A}\)
533	    (locally finite type, flat, etc.), not unconditional consequences of the construction.
534	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety structure} (A.3) --- a
535	    separate Route~A sub-row, fed by but not constructed in this chapter.
536	\end{itemize}

32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
36	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
37	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
38	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
39	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
40	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
41	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
42	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
43	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness; see~\)
44	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
45	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
46	
47	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1 and
48	% \S 5; FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
49	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500 +
50	% L2154-L2542). The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
51	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
52	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
53	% the verbatim source-quote in \ is taken from
54	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
55	% reference).
56	
57	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
58	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
59	
60	\begin{definition}\leanok
61	[Hilbert polynomial]
62	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
63	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
64	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
65	  % Polynomials" (read from
66	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
67	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
68	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
69	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
70	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
71	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
72	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
73	  %  the function
74	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
75	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
76	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
77	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
78	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
79	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
80	  %
81	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
82	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
83	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
84	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
85	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
86	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
87	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
88	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
89	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
90	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
91	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
92	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
93	  III.5.2.}
94	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
95	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
96	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), the
97	  \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\) relative to \(L\)} is the
98	  polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) characterised by
99	  \[
100	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
101	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
102	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
103	  \]
104	  (a polynomial in \(m\) by Snapper's Lemma; the sum is finite by coherence and
105	  properness). When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
106	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
107	  well-defined on connected components.
108	\end{definition}
